3(4-1) &= 3 \times 3 = 9 \\
2(4) &= 8 \\
M &= 9 - 8 - 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude:**

$$
M = 1
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A mobility of **1** means the mechanism has **one input** (e.g., the wiper motor rotation) and the rest of the motion is fully determined — this is the desired deterministic behavior.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Slider-Crank Mechanism (Internal Combustion Engine Piston)

**Problem:** A slider-crank has n = 4 links (ground, crank, connecting rod, slider), j₁ = 4 revolute joints, j₂ = 0.

**Step 1 — Apply the planar DOF equation:**

$$
M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Position of the piston (slider displacement x) as a function of crank angle θ:**

Let crank length = **r**, connecting rod length = **l**, and θ = crank angle from TDC.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(\theta) &= r\cos\theta + \sqrt{l^2 - (r\sin\theta)^2} \\
\text{(using slider along horizontal axis, origin at crank center)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Piston velocity by chain rule:**

$$
v(\theta) = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{dx}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{dx}{d\theta} \cdot \omega
$$

$$
\frac{dx}{d\theta} = -r\sin\theta - \frac{r^2 \sin\theta \cos\theta}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2 \sin^2\theta}}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering use in simulation:** In SolidWorks Motion Study or CATIA Kinematics, the user inputs **ω = 3000 RPM** (engine speed), and the software integrates these equations numerically (RK4) to plot piston velocity vs. crank angle. This is the *exact same* computation the CAD solver performs.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: STL Triangle Count for a Closed Manifold Mesh (Euler's Formula)

**Problem:** An STL file describes a 3D solid using only triangles. For a valid closed mesh, the number of triangles is related to vertices and faces.

**Step 1 — Euler's polyhedron formula for a genus-0 closed mesh:**

$$
V - E + F = 2
$$

where $V$ = vertices, $E$ = edges, $F$ = faces (triangles).

**Step 2 — For triangular faces only, each triangle has 3 edges, and each edge is shared by exactly 2 triangles:**

$$
E = \frac{3F}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into Euler's equation:**

$$
V - \frac{3F}{2} + F = 2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V - \frac{F}{2} &= 2 \\
V &= 2 + \frac{F}{2} \\
F &= 2(V - 2) = 2V - 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Result:**

$$
\boxed{N_t = 2V - 4}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering takeaway:** A higher STL resolution produces exponentially more triangles → larger file size → slower slicing. KTU tip: default chordal deviation of **0.01 mm** in CAD export gives an acceptable balance for FDM printing.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Simulation Result Interpretation

| Result Type | What it tells you | Typical use |
|---|---|---|
| **Interference detection** | Two solid bodies overlap at some frame | Find collision, redesign clearance |
| **Trace path** | Locus of a point/edge/vertex over time | Ergonomic reach analysis |
| **Displacement plot** | Position vs. time of any vertex | Check maximum travel |
| **Velocity plot** | Velocity vs. time | Find peak speed, motor sizing |
| **Acceleration plot** | Acceleration vs. time | Vibration, inertial loads |
| **Contact / cam profile** | Generates cam from follower trace | Reverse-engineer cam shape |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Kinematic Simulation Pipeline (Lab Procedure)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: CAD Part Models] --> B[Open Assembly Module]
    B --> C[Import All Component Parts]
    C --> D[Apply Mates: Coincident, Concentric, Tangent]
    D --> E[Verify Gruebler DOF = 1]
    E --> F[Add Motion Driver: Motor or Linear Actuator]
    F --> G[Define Time: t = 0 to 5 s, Step dt = 0.01 s]
    G --> H[Run Solver: Newton-Raphson Iterations]
    H --> I{Solver Converged?}
    I -- No --> J[Refine Mates or Reduce dt]
    J --> F
    I -- Yes --> K[Plot Displacement, Velocity, Acceleration]
    K --> L[Detect Interferences]
    L --> M{Interference Found?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Redesign Geometry, Return to Part Module]
    N --> A
    M -- No --> O[Export Animation as .avi or .mp4]
    O --> P[Export STL for 3D Printing]
    P --> Q[End: Validated Mechanism]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow: 3D Printing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Validated CAD Model] --> B[Export as STL]
    B --> C[Import STL into Slicer Software e.g. Cura]
    C --> D[Set Print Parameters]
    D --> D1[Layer Height: 0.2 mm]
    D --> D2[Infill: 20 percent]
    D --> D3[Supports: Tree or Grid]
    D --> D4[Orientation: Flat on Bed]
    D --> E[Slicer Generates G-code]
    E --> F[Transfer G-code to Printer via SD or USB]
    F --> G[Printer Bed Leveling]
    G --> H[Heat Nozzle and Bed to Target Temperatures]
    H --> I[Layer by Layer Deposition Begins]
    I --> J[Print Completion]
    J --> K[Cool Down Part on Bed]
    K --> L[Remove Part and Supports]
    L --> M[Post Processing: Sanding, Painting]
    M --> N[Final Functional Prototype]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Joint Type Classification in CAD Simulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Joints in Kinematic Simulation] --> B[Lower Pair 1 DOF]
    A --> C[Lower Pair 2 DOF]
    A --> D[Higher Pair]
    B --> B1[Revolute Hinge]
    B --> B2[Prismatic Slider]
    B --> B3[Screw Helical]
    B --> B4[Cylindrical]
    C --> C1[Planar 2DOF]
    C --> C2[Spherical 3DOF]
    D --> D1[Cam Follower]
    D --> D2[Gear Pair]
    D --> D3[Belt or Chain]
    D --> D4[Rolling Contact]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block: Comparison of 3D Printing Technologies (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> C[Required Accuracy]
    A --> D[Budget Constraint]

    B --> B3[Metal Powder]
    B --> B4[Nylon Powder]
    C --> C1[Standard 0.2 mm]
    C --> C2[High 0.05 mm]
    C --> C3[Ultra 0.02 mm]
    D --> D1[Low Cost FDM]
    D --> D2[Medium Cost SLA]
    D --> D3[High Cost SLS]
    D --> D4[Industrial DMLS]
    B1 --> R1[Use FDM Printer]
    B2 --> R2[Use SLA Printer]
    B3 --> R3[Use DMLS Printer]
    B4 --> R4[Use SLS Printer]
    C2 --> R2
    C3 --> R2
```
