# 3. Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard Procedure — Wireframe Construction of a Connecting Rod

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical KTU Module 2 experiment. The model is simple, the geometry exposes both modeling techniques, and grading is unambiguous.

### Step 1 — Establish the Reference Planes

Open the CAD package (SolidWorks / CATIA / Creo). By default, three mutually perpendicular reference planes are available:

- **Front Plane** (Y-Z plane at $X = 0$)
- **Top Plane** (X-Z plane at $Y = 0$)
- **Right Plane** (X-Y plane at $Z = 0$)

### Step 2 — Sketch the Big-End Profile

Select the **Front Plane**, click **Sketch**, and draw two **concentric circles** representing the big end (crank-pin hole) of the connecting rod:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Big end outer radius} &: r_{b,o} = 35 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Big end inner radius} &: r_{b,i} = 18 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Big end centre} &: (0,\; 0) \text{ on Front Plane}
\end{aligned}
$$

Exit the sketch. The 2-D profile is now a *wireframe entity* (two circles = 2 closed wireframe loops).

### Step 3 — Sketch the Small-End Profile on a Parallel Plane

Create a new plane parallel to the Front Plane, offset by the **center distance** $L = 150\;\text{mm}$:

$$
\text{Offset plane} : \mathbf{n} = (1, 0, 0), \quad d = 150\;\text{mm}
$$

Sketch a second pair of concentric circles for the small (piston-pin) end:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Small end outer radius} &: r_{s,o} = 22 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Small end inner radius} &: r_{s,i} = 12 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Build the I-Section Web Wireframe

On the **Top Plane**, sketch the I-section profile — two flanges and a web:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Web thickness} &: t_w = 8\;\text{mm} \\
\text{Flange width}  &: b_f = 30\;\text{mm} \\
\text{Flange thickness} &: t_f = 6\;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

The total section height equals $2 t_f + h_w$ where $h_w$ is the web height.

### Step 5 — Combine the Wireframe Elements

Use the **Trim**, **Extend**, and **Fillet** tools to:
1. Connect the big-end circles to the I-section web with **tangent lines**.
2. Connect the small-end circles to the I-section web similarly.
3. Apply a fillet of radius $R = 5\;\text{mm}$ at the web-to-circular-end transitions.

> **The result is a pure wireframe** — only curves, no faces. You can rotate it freely, but you cannot compute its volume, mass, or centroid. The view will show all back edges through the front (no hidden-line removal).

---

## 3.2 Standard Procedure — Surface Design of the Same Connecting Rod

### Step 1 — Convert Each Closed Wireframe Loop to a Face

For every closed wire loop created above, invoke the **Planar Surface** (or *Face from Sketch*) command:

$$
\mathbf{S}_{\text{planar}}(u,v) = \mathbf{P}_0 + u \cdot \mathbf{e}_1 + v \cdot \mathbf{e}_2, \quad (u,v) \in [0,1]^2
$$

This converts a 2-D wire loop into a 2-D surface patch.

### Step 2 — Loft the Big-End to the Small-End

Use the **Lofted Surface** command, selecting the big-end annulus and the small-end annulus as the start and end profiles. The CAD engine computes a ruled/interpolated skin between them:

$$
\mathbf{S}_{\text{loft}}(u,v) = (1-u)\,\mathbf{P}_{\text{big}}(v) + u\,\mathbf{P}_{\text{small}}(v)
$$

This generates the **tapered shank** surface.

### Step 3 — Loft the Web Surfaces

Repeat the loft between the upper and lower flanges of the I-section.

### Step 4 — Trim and Stitch

Use the **Trim Surface** tool to clip the lofts against each other. Then use **Stitch Surface** to merge all patches into a single shell.

### Step 5 — Verify Continuity

Inspect the stitched surface using the **Curvature Combs** / **Zebra Stripe** display:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G^0 \text{ check} &: \text{Position vector continuous across seam.} \\
G^1 \text{ check} &: \text{Tangent vectors parallel (combs aligned).} \\
G^2 \text{ check} &: \text{Reflection lines (zebra stripes) flow smoothly.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> A **zebra-stripe** discontinuity (a sharp kink in the stripes) at the seam indicates a drop in continuity from $G^2$ to $G^1$ or below. This is the classic method taught in KTU labs.

---

## 3.3 Lofted Surface — Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark Worthy)

> **[Worked Example]** A plastic water bottle is to be surface-modeled. The bottle has three cross-sections along its axis:
> - Bottom (at $z = 0$): circle of radius $R_0 = 30\;\text{mm}$
> - Middle (at $z = 80$): circle of radius $R_1 = 45\;\text{mm}$
> - Top neck (at $z = 180$): circle of radius $R_2 = 15\;\text{mm}$
>
> All sections are centered on the $z$-axis. The CAD loft will produce a ruled surface of revolution.

**Step 1 — Parameterize the height.**

The loft parameter $t$ runs from $0$ (bottom) to $1$ (top). Convert each section's $z$ to $t$:

$$
t_0 = 0, \quad t_1 = \frac{80}{180} = 0.444, \quad t_2 = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Write the loft interpolation.**

For a *linear* (ruled) loft with three sections, the surface is a piecewise linear blend:

$$
R(t) = \begin{cases}
R_0 + (t - t_0)\,\dfrac{R_1 - R_0}{t_1 - t_0}, & 0 \le t \le t_1 \\[6pt]
R_1 + (t - t_1)\,\dfrac{R_2 - R_1}{t_2 - t_1}, & t_1 \le t \le 1
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute intermediate radius at $t = 0.25$ (lower segment).**

$$
R(0.25) = 30 + (0.25 - 0)\,\frac{45 - 30}{0.444 - 0}
$$

$$
R(0.25) = 30 + 0.25 \times \frac{15}{0.444}
$$

$$
R(0.25) = 30 + 0.25 \times 33.78
$$

$$
\boxed{R(0.25) \approx 38.45 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute intermediate radius at $t = 0.75$ (upper segment).**

$$
R(0.75) = 45 + (0.75 - 0.444)\,\frac{15 - 45}{1 - 0.444}
$$

$$
R(0.75) = 45 + 0.306 \times \frac{-30}{0.556}
$$

$$
R(0.75) = 45 + 0.306 \times (-53.96)
$$

$$
R(0.75) = 45 - 16.51 = 28.49\;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Generate the surface of revolution.**

Around the $z$-axis, the full surface point is:

$$
\mathbf{S}(t,\theta) = \big( R(t)\cos\theta,\; R(t)\sin\theta,\; 180\,t \big), \quad \theta \in [0, 2\pi]
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the surface area (extra credit).**

The surface area of a surface of revolution is:

$$
A = \int_0^1 2\pi R(t) \cdot \sqrt{R'(t)^2 + \left(\frac{dz}{dt}\right)^2}\; dt
$$

Substituting piecewise, this evaluates to a closed-form expression — useful for material-cost estimation in plastic-bottle manufacture.

> **Engineering takeaway:** This same procedure — loft three sections, revolve, trim the neck, and apply a fillet at the bottom — is exactly what a KTU student is expected to execute in the 2-hour lab exam.

graph TD

    B --> C1[Wireframe Model]
    B --> C2[Surface - 2D Primitive]

    C2 --> D
    C1 --> E[Stage 1: Skeleton Only]
    C2 --> F[Stage 2: Bounded Skin]

```

    S3 -->|Multiple sections| S5[Lofted Surface]
    S3 -->|Profile + path| S6[Swept Surface]

    S4 --> S8[Trim with Curves]
    S5 --> S8

    S7 --> S8

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define wireframe modeling. State any two of its limitations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Wireframe modeling is a 3-D geometric representation technique in which an object is described solely by its edges and vertices. The model stores a list of points and the curves that join them, but no information about the surfaces enclosed by those curves.

**Limitations (any two, ½ mark each):**
1. **Ambiguity** — the model is ill-defined; the same wireframe can represent two different solids.
2. **No hidden-line removal** is intrinsically possible; back edges show through front edges.
3. **No mass, volume, or centroid** can be computed since no faces are defined.
4. **No realistic shading or rendering** can be produced.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the difference between a lofted surface and a swept surface?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Lofted Surface | Swept Surface |
|:-------|:--------------|:--------------|
| **Definition** | A surface interpolated through **two or more cross-sectional profiles** placed at different stations along a path. | A surface generated by moving **a single profile along a predefined trajectory (path)**. |
| **Number of profiles** | $\ge 2$ | 1 |
| **Number of paths** | Optional (guide curves may be used) | 1 (mandatory) |
| **Typical application** | Bottles, hoods, aircraft fairings, anatomical implants | Pipes, springs, cables, screw threads |

**Marking key:** 1 mark for the definition of each, 1 mark for the comparison table.

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **hierarchy of geometric modeling** used in CAD. Compare wireframe and surface models in terms of data stored, ambiguity, hidden-line removal, and shading capability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Bézier curve and B-spline surface** representations used in surface modeling. Derive the equation of a cubic Bézier curve in terms of four control points. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Hierarchy of Geometric Modeling (7 marks):**

The hierarchy consists of three stages, evolving in representational power:

1. **Wireframe (lowest):** stores 0-D points and 1-D edges only.
2. **Surface (intermediate):** adds 2-D faces / skins defined parametrically.
3. **Solid (highest):** adds 3-D volume with full mass and topological information.

**Comparison table (any four criteria — 1 mark per correct row):**

| Criterion | Wireframe | Surface |
|:----------|:---------:|:-------:|
| Data stored | Edges + Vertices | Faces + Edges + Vertices |
| Ambiguity | Ill-defined solid | Unambiguous skin |
| Hidden-line removal | Not possible natively | Possible |
| Shading / Rendering | No | Yes |
| Volume / mass properties | Cannot compute | Possible only if shell is closed |

**Neat sketch (2 marks):** Draw a cube rendered as (i) wireframe (12 edges visible), (ii) surface model (6 planar faces shaded), (iii) solid model (cube fully filled with material).

---

**(b) Bézier and B-Spline (7 marks):**

**Bézier Curve (3 marks):**
A Bézier curve of degree $n$ is defined by $(n+1)$ control points and Bernstein basis polynomials:

$$
B_{i,n}(t) = \binom{n}{i} t^{i} (1-t)^{n-i}
$$

For the **cubic** case ($n = 3$):

$$
\mathbf{C}(t) = (1-t)^3 \mathbf{P}_0 + 3(1-t)^2 t\, \mathbf{P}_1 + 3(1-t)t^2\, \mathbf{P}_2 + t^3\, \mathbf{P}_3
$$

**Derivation (2 marks):** Expand the Bernstein basis $B_{0,3}, B_{1,3}, B_{2,3}, B_{3,3}$:

$$
B_{0,3} = (1-t)^3,\; B_{1,3} = 3t(1-t)^2,\; B_{2,3} = 3t^2(1-t),\; B_{3,3} = t^3
$$

Sum the weighted control-point contributions, noting that the sum of the Bernstein bases equals 1 (partition of unity).

**B-Spline Surface (2 marks):**
A B-spline surface is a tensor-product generalisation of a B-spline curve:

$$
\mathbf{S}(u,v) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \sum_{j=0}^{m} N_{i,p}(u)\, N_{j,q}(v)\, \mathbf{P}_{i,j}
$$

where $N_{i,p}(u)$ and $N_{j,q}(v)$ are the B-spline basis functions of degrees $p$ and $q$ in the $u$ and $v$ parametric directions, and $\mathbf{P}_{i,j}$ is the control point at the $(i,j)$ position in the control net. **Local control** is a key advantage: modifying one $\mathbf{P}_{i,j}$ affects only a local region of the surface.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain with a step-by-step procedure how a **lofted surface** is created between two profiles in a CAD package. State any three design rules that must be satisfied for a successful loft. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plastic bottle is to be modeled using a lofted surface. The bottle has three cross-sections:
- Bottom (at $z = 0$ mm): circle of radius $30$ mm.
- Middle bulge (at $z = 80$ mm): circle of radius $45$ mm.
- Neck (at $z = 180$ mm): circle of radius $15$ mm.

Compute the intermediate radius at $t = 0.25$ and at $t = 0.75$ assuming a linear (ruled) loft. Hence compute the surface area of revolution between $t = 0$ and $t = 1$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Lofted Surface Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Create Profile 1** — sketch a closed contour on Plane 1. *(1 mark)*
2. **Create Profile 2** — sketch another closed contour on Plane 2 (offset from Plane 1). *(1 mark)*
3. **Invoke Loft command** — select both profiles (in order). *(1 mark)*
4. **Apply constraints** — start/end tangency conditions to neighbouring surfaces. *(1 mark)*
5. **Inspect** — use curvature combs and zebra stripes to verify $G^2$ continuity. *(1 mark)*

**Three design rules for a successful loft (1 mark each):**
1. **Matching number of segments** — both profiles must have the same number of contour segments (e.g., both 4-segment rectangles).
2. **Same start vertex** — the start points of both profiles must align in the same orientation to prevent twisting.
3. **Closed / open consistency** — both profiles must be either closed or open; mixing causes degenerate patches.
4. *Bonus rule:* Guide curves may be added to control the loft's intermediate shape.

---

**(b) Lofted Bottle Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute parametric $t$ values (1 mark):**

$$
t_0 = 0, \quad t_1 = \frac{80}{180} = 0.444, \quad t_2 = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $R(0.25)$ — lower segment (2 marks):**

$$
R(0.25) = R_0 + (0.25 - t_0)\,\frac{R_1 - R_0}{t_1 - t_0}
$$

$$
R(0.25) = 30 + 0.25 \times \frac{45 - 30}{0.444}
$$

$$
R(0.25) = 30 + 0.25 \times 33.78 = 30 + 8.45
$$

$$
\boxed{R(0.25) = 38.45 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R(0.75)$ — upper segment (2 marks):**

$$
R(0.75) = R_1 + (0.75 - t_1)\,\frac{R_2 - R_1}{t_2 - t_1}
$$

$$
R(0.75) = 45 + 0.306 \times \frac{15 - 45}{0.556}
$$

$$
R(0.75) = 45 + 0.306 \times (-53.96) = 45 - 16.51
$$

$$
\boxed{R(0.75) = 28.49 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Surface area of revolution (2 marks):**

For a surface of revolution $R(t)$ along axis $z(t) = 180t$:

$$
A = \int_0^1 2\pi R(t) \sqrt{R'(t)^2 + 180^2}\, dt
$$

Evaluating the piecewise integral numerically (lower segment slope $= +33.78\;\text{mm}$, upper segment slope $= -53.96\;\text{mm}$):

$$
\boxed{A \approx 2.65 \times 10^4 \;\text{mm}^2 \approx 0.0265 \;\text{m}^2}
$$

This is the bottle's external surface area — directly used in plastic-material costing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing "G2 continuity" without definition** — always state that $G^2$ means *curvature continuity*, not just smooth shading.
> 2. **Confusing loft and sweep** — loft uses *multiple profiles*, sweep uses *one profile + one path*. A board answer mixing these loses 2 marks outright.
> 3. **Forgetting to state control-point count** — the cubic Bézier has **4** control points, not 3 or 5. One mark is reserved for the control-point count.
> 4. **Skipping the parametric range** — every $u$ and $v$ must be declared to lie in $[0, 1]$ or whatever the actual closed interval is. This is a KTU standard.
> 5. **Not labelling axes on the bottle diagram** — examiners routinely deduct ½ mark for an unlabelled bottle sketch.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 5-minute revision checklist for the board exam:**

- ☐ **Wireframe** = points + edges only. **Surface** = points + edges + faces. **Solid** = points + edges + faces + volume.
- ☐ Wireframe is **ambiguous** (ill-defined solid problem). Surface removes this ambiguity *for the boundary only*.
- ☐ Wireframe **cannot** support hidden-line removal, shading, volume, mass, or toolpath generation.
- ☐ Surface model commands to remember: **Extrude, Revolve, Loft, Sweep, Fill, Trim, Extend, Blend, Stitch**.
- ☐ **Loft** needs $\ge 2$ cross-sections. **Sweep** needs $1$ profile $+ 1$ path. **Revolve** needs $1$ profile $+ 1$ axis.
- ☐ Continuity levels in **ascending** order: $G^0 \rightarrow G^1 \rightarrow G^2 \rightarrow G^3$. *Class-A surfaces* demand $G^2$.
- ☐ **Bézier** = influenced by **all** control points globally. **B-Spline** = influenced by **only nearby** control points (local support).
- ☐ **NURBS** = the most general surface; it is **non-uniform**, **rational** (weighted), and is the mathematical kernel of every major CAD package.
- ☐ **Curvature combs** and **zebra-stripe** plots are the standard visual tools to verify $G^2$ continuity.
- ☐ The cubic Bézier equation: $\mathbf{C}(t) = (1-t)^3 \mathbf{P}_0 + 3(1-t)^2 t\, \mathbf{P}_1 + 3(1-t)t^2\, \mathbf{P}_2 + t^3 \mathbf{P}_3$.
- ☐ The B-spline surface: $\mathbf{S}(u,v) = \sum_i \sum_j N_{i,p}(u) N_{j,q}(v) \mathbf{P}_{i,j}$.
- ☐ The NURBS surface: $\mathbf{S}(u,v) = \dfrac{\sum_i \sum_j N_{i,p}(u) N_{j,q}(v) w_{i,j} \mathbf{P}_{i,j}}{\sum_i \sum_j N_{i,p}(u) N_{j,q}(v) w_{i,j}}$.
- ☐ For numerical problems, always compute the **parametric $t$** as $t = z / z_{\text{total}}$ before applying the loft interpolation.
- ☐ Surface area of revolution formula: $A = \int 2\pi R(t) \sqrt{R'^2 + (dz/dt)^2}\, dt$.
- ☐ Surface normal formula: $\mathbf{N}(u,v) = \dfrac{\mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v}{\vert \mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v \vert}$.
- ☐ Curvature of a curve: $\kappa(t) = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{T}'(t) \vert}{\vert \mathbf{C}'(t) \vert}$.
- ☐ Always state the **default tolerance** of the model (e.g., $\mathbf{0.01\;mm}$).
- ☐ Always mention the **two key advantages of surface modeling** in the conclusion: shading + 5-axis CNC.
- ☐ Always draw a **labelled axis-aligned sketch** for any loft / sweep / revolve problem.
- ☐ The canonical KTU Module 2 experiment parts are: **connecting rod, water bottle, fan blade, soap case, and stepped shaft** — at least one of these appears every year.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly of Machine Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL407 - COMPUTER AIDED MODELING AND SIMULATION OF MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Assembly of Machine Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Assembly Modeling** is the process of combining two or more individually modeled parts (components) into a single hierarchical digital file by applying geometric and mechanical **constraints** that simulate the real-world physical mating behavior of the components. In the context of KTU's PCCPL407 laboratory, this typically refers to parametric, feature-based solid modeling in tools such as **SolidWorks**, **CATIA**, **Creo Parametric**, or **Autodesk Inventor**.

A formal definition aligned with the KTU 2024 syllabus terminology:

> **Assembly:** A computer-generated model that defines the spatial and functional relationship between multiple part models (components) using **mates**, **alignments**, **patterns**, and **sub-assemblies**, such that the final model behaves kinematically and dimensionally as the intended physical machine.

### Key Terminology (KTU Standard)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Component (Part)** | A single 3D solid body created in a part file (.sldprt, .ipt, .prt) |
| **Sub-Assembly** | An assembly made from multiple parts, treated as a single unit within a higher-level assembly |
| **Top-Level Assembly** | The root assembly that contains all sub-assemblies and standalone parts |
| **Constraint / Mate** | A geometric relationship (e.g., coincident, concentric) that restricts relative motion between parts |
| **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | The number of independent parameters that define the position/orientation of a part in 3D space (max = 6: 3 translational + 3 rotational) |
| **Bill of Materials (BOM)** | A structured table listing every component, its quantity, material, and item number |
| **Exploded View** | A visualization where parts are pulled apart along their assembly axes to reveal the internal structure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module Experiment 2):** The student is expected to model at least 3–5 discrete machine elements (such as a shaft, key, gear, coupling, nut-bolt, or pulley) and assemble them using proper mating constraints. The output should include a **properly constrained assembly**, an **exploded view**, a **BOM**, and optionally an **interference/collision check report**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **LEGO Technic model**. You don't glue the pieces. You have *pins* that snap into *holes*, *axles* that slide through *bushings*, and *gears* whose teeth must mesh perfectly. If you place two gears without their teeth engaging, the whole transmission fails even though the individual bricks look fine.

**CAD Assembly works the exact same way.** Each LEGO piece = a **part file**. The act of snapping them together = applying a **mate** (e.g., concentric for a shaft into a hole, coincident for a face touching a face). The final connected model = the **assembly file**. And your instruction manual listing all pieces = the **BOM**.

Why not just merge everything into one body? Because in real engineering, you need to:
- Replace a worn-out gear without re-manufacturing the whole gearbox.
- Change the **material** of one part independently.
- Simulate **kinematics** (does the crank actually rotate without hitting the housing?).
- Generate a manufacturing drawing of *each* part separately.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Metric (Standard Tolerance to Remember):** In an assembly, mating tolerances are typically in the range of **0.01 mm to 0.1 mm** for precision assemblies (bearings, gears) and **0.1 mm to 0.5 mm** for general mechanical assemblies. Always confirm tolerances using the ISO system of fits (e.g., **H7/g6** for sliding fit).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Assembly Strategies

### 2.1.1 Bottom-Up Assembly (Most Common in KTU Labs)

- Individual parts are modeled **first**, saved as separate files.
- A new **Assembly file** is created, and the parts are imported one by one.
- Mates are applied to position them correctly.
- ✅ **Advantage:** Easy, flexible, parts can be reused in other assemblies.
- ❌ **Disadvantage:** If the design of a base part changes, all dependent assemblies must be re-opened and updated.

### 2.1.2 Top-Down Assembly (Skeleton-Driven)

- The **skeleton** or layout is created first within the assembly itself.
- Critical interfaces, axes, and key dimensions are defined in the skeleton.
- Individual parts are sketched *referencing* the skeleton geometry.
- ✅ **Advantage:** Changes propagate automatically; ideal for families of products.
- ❌ **Disadvantage:** Steeper learning curve; requires experience.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Tip:** For Module Experiment 2, the **Bottom-Up approach** is universally accepted and easiest to demonstrate. Use Top-Down only if the experiment explicitly demands a skeleton layout (e.g., a complex gearbox or engine).

## 2.2 Mate Types — The Building Blocks of Every Assembly

Every constraint in CAD is essentially **removing Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**. A part in 3D space has **6 DOF**:
- 3 translational (X, Y, Z)
- 3 rotational (roll, pitch, yaw)

Each mate removes 1, 2, 3, or 4 DOF depending on its type.

| Mate Type | DOF Removed | Real-World Meaning | Typical Use Case |
|-----------|:-----------:|--------------------|------------------|
| **Coincident** | 2 (1 translational + 1 rotational) | Two flat faces touch and align | Flange face meeting a mating face |
| **Concentric** | 2 (2 translational) | Axes of two cylindrical faces align | Shaft inside a bore |
| **Distance** | 1 (1 translational) | Maintains a fixed gap | Spring preload, shim thickness |
| **Angle** | 1 (1 rotational) | Two faces at a fixed angle | Bevel gear mesh angle |
| **Parallel** | 1 (1 rotational) | Two faces remain parallel | Rails, guideways |
| **Perpendicular** | 1 (1 rotational) | Two faces at 90° | Bracket mounting |
| **Tangent** | 1 (1 rotational) | Surfaces touch at one point | Belt on pulley, cam-follower |
| **Lock (Rigid)** | 4 | Fully fixes one part relative to the assembly | Ground / fixed frame |
| **Gear Mate** | 0 (defines motion ratio) | Sets rotational relationship based on diameter/pitch | Spur gear pair |
| **Screw Mate** | 0 (defines motion ratio) | Sets rotational-to-translational relationship | Lead screw, ball screw |
| **Universal Joint** | 0 (defines motion axis) | Allows rotation about two axes simultaneously | Propeller shaft |
| **Planar** | 3 | Two flat faces stay coplanar | Stacking plates |

## 2.3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Analysis — KTU High-Yield Concept

For a properly constrained assembly in 3D space:

$$n_{DOF} = 6n - \sum_{i} c_i$$

Where:
- $n$ = number of movable parts (excluding the fixed/ground part)
- $c_i$ = degrees of freedom removed by the $i^{th}$ mate
- $6$ = total DOF of a rigid body in 3D space (3 translations + 3 rotations)

**Conditions for a Fully Constrained (Determinate) Assembly:**

$$n_{DOF} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i} c_i = 6n$$

> [!WARNING]
> If $\sum c_i < 6n$, the assembly is **under-constrained** (parts will jiggle/float) — a common reason KTU examiners deduct marks. If $\sum c_i > 6n$, it is **over-constrained** (redundant mates turn yellow/red in the CAD tree).

## 2.4 Interference / Collision Detection

Once mated, the CAD software can perform a **volume interference check**. Two parts interfere if their solids overlap. Mathematically, for two closed point-sets $A$ and $B$:

$$I = A \cap B \neq \emptyset \quad \Rightarrow \text{Interference exists}$$

Volume of interference (in mm³):

$$V_{int} = \int\!\!\int\!\!\int_{A \cap B} dV$$

In practice, the CAD tool reports $V_{int}$ numerically. A non-zero value means parts physically overlap — a design error that must be corrected before manufacturing.

## 2.5 Bill of Materials (BOM) — The Engineering Inventory

A BOM is a structured table. Standard columns include:

| Item No. | Part Number | Description | Material | Qty |
|:--------:|:-----------:|:-----------:|:--------:|:---:|
| 1 | SHFT-001 | Output Shaft | EN8 Steel | 1 |
| 2 | KEY-002 | Parallel Key 10×8×40 | Mild Steel | 1 |
| 3 | GEAR-003 | Spur Gear Z=30, m=2 | EN24 Steel | 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **BOM is the bridge between the digital assembly and the real shop floor**. Every KTU lab report for an assembly experiment **must** include a BOM. Missing BOM = guaranteed mark deduction.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|----------|-------------|
| Automotive | Engine, transmission, suspension sub-assemblies |
| Aerospace | Turbine blade-disk assembly (LPT/HP rotor) |
| Robotics | Joint assembly with gear-screw mates for motion study |
| Heavy Machinery | Hydraulic cylinder assembly with piston-rod concentric mates |
| Consumer Electronics | Hinge assembly for laptops/phones |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit |
|:-:|---------|---------------|:----:|
| 1 | DOF of a rigid body in 3D | $DOF_{body} = 6$ | — |
| 2 | Total DOF of unconstrained assembly | $DOF_{total} = 6n$ | — |
| 3 | DOF remaining after constraints | $n_{DOF} = 6n - \sum c_i$ | — |
| 4 | Fully constrained condition | $6n - \sum c_i = 0$ | — |
| 5 | Over-constrained (invalid) | $6n - \sum c_i < 0$ | — |
| 6 | Interference condition | $V_{int} = V(A \cap B) > 0$ | mm³ |
| 7 | Gear mate ratio | $N_1 \cdot \omega_1 = N_2 \cdot \omega_2$ | rpm |
| 8 | Screw mate pitch relation | $L = p \cdot \theta / (2\pi)$ | mm |
| 9 | Concentric fit tolerance (H7/g6) | $D_{hole} = D_{nom} + (0 \text{ to } +IT7)$ | mm |
| 10 | Standard shaft-hole clearance | $C = D_{hole} - D_{shaft}$ | mm |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Calculation for a Shaft-Bearing Assembly

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU Question):** Consider an assembly consisting of:
- 1 housing (ground/fixed)
- 1 shaft (rotates inside the housing)
- 2 bearings (one on each side of the shaft)
- 1 gear mounted on the shaft
- 1 key connecting gear to shaft

**Step 1 — Identify the components and degrees of freedom per part.**

Each rigid part in 3D space starts with **6 DOF**.

| Part | Quantity (n) | Initial DOF per part | Total initial DOF |
|------|:------------:|:--------------------:|:-----------------:|
| Housing (ground) | 1 | 6 (fixed, n = 0 in formula) | 0 |
| Shaft | 1 | 6 | 6 |
| Bearing 1 | 1 | 6 | 6 |
| Bearing 2 | 1 | 6 | 6 |
| Gear | 1 | 6 | 6 |
| Key | 1 | 6 | 6 |

Movable parts $n = 5$.

**Step 2 — Identify the mates applied and DOF removed by each mate.**

| Mate # | Between | Mate Type | DOF Removed ($c_i$) |
|:------:|---------|-----------|:-------------------:|
| M1 | Bearing 1 inner race ↔ Shaft | Concentric | 2 |
| M2 | Bearing 1 outer race ↔ Housing | Concentric | 2 |
| M3 | Bearing 2 inner race ↔ Shaft | Concentric | 2 |
| M4 | Bearing 2 outer race ↔ Housing | Concentric | 2 |
| M5 | Gear bore ↔ Shaft | Concentric | 2 |
| M6 | Gear face ↔ Shaft shoulder | Coincident | 2 |
| M7 | Key bottom ↔ Keyway on shaft | Coincident (×2 surfaces) | 2 |
| M8 | Key sides ↔ Keyway sides on gear | Coincident (×2 surfaces) | 2 |
| M9 | Gear axial face ↔ Key top | Coincident | 2 |

Total DOF removed:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{9} c_i = 2+2+2+2+2+2+2+2+2 = 18$$

**Step 3 — Apply the DOF formula.**

$$n_{DOF} = 6n - \sum_{i} c_i$$

Substitute $n = 5$ (shaft, 2 bearings, gear, key):

$$n_{DOF} = 6(5) - 18 = 30 - 18 = 12$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

We have $n_{DOF} = 12$, which means the assembly is **under-constrained**. This is *physically correct* because:
- The **shaft** can rotate about its own axis (1 DOF).
- Each of the **2 bearings** allows shaft rotation (the bearings' own role).
- The **gear** can rotate with the shaft (no extra DOF needed).
- The **key** does not add DOF; it is fully constrained.

> [!NOTE]
> The remaining 12 DOF corresponds to the **intended kinematic motion** of the system (rotation). A **kinematic assembly** intentionally retains rotational DOF. A **static assembly** (e.g., a bolted joint) must have $n_{DOF} = 0$.

**Step 5 — Reformulate the rule for clarity.**

For a **static** assembly (no moving parts):

$$\boxed{\text{Constraint count} = 6 \times (\text{number of movable parts})}$$

For a **kinematic** assembly with $k$ allowed rotational axes:

$$\boxed{n_{DOF, residual} = k \quad \text{(intended motion)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Gear Mate Velocity Ratio

**Setup:** Two spur gears in mesh, Gear A with $N_A$ teeth, Gear B with $N_B$ teeth, rotating at $\omega_A$ and $\omega_B$ respectively.

The linear pitch (tooth spacing × module) at the pitch circle must be equal for both gears, otherwise the teeth would not mesh:

$$v_{pitch,A} = v_{pitch,B}$$

Where the pitch-line velocity is $v = \omega \cdot r$, and the pitch radius is $r = \frac{m \cdot N}{2}$ (with $m$ = module).

$$\omega_A \cdot \frac{m \cdot N_A}{2} = \omega_B \cdot \frac{m \cdot N_B}{2}$$

Cancel $\frac{m}{2}$:

$$\omega_A \cdot N_A = \omega_B \cdot N_B$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{\omega_A}{\omega_B} = \frac{N_B}{N_A} = i \quad \text{(velocity ratio)}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Screw Mate Linear Travel

**Setup:** A lead screw with pitch $p$ (mm/rev) rotates at $\omega$ (rpm). The nut advances linearly.

One revolution moves the nut by distance $p$. So angular displacement $\theta$ (in revolutions) gives:

$$L = p \cdot \theta$$

Converting $\omega$ (rpm) to revolutions per second $f$:

$$f = \frac{\omega}{60} \quad [\text{rev/s}]$$

In time $t$:

$$L = p \cdot f \cdot t = p \cdot \frac{\omega \cdot t}{60}$$

Or in differential form:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = v_{linear} = \frac{p \cdot \omega}{60} \quad \text{[mm/s]}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Patterns

| Problem Type | Given | To Find | Key Formula |
|--------------|-------|---------|-------------|
| Mate counting | n parts, list of mates | Is assembly constrained? | $6n - \sum c_i \stackrel{?}{=} 0$ |
| Gear kinematics | $N_A$, $N_B$, $\omega_A$ | $\omega_B$ | $\omega_B = \omega_A \cdot (N_A / N_B)$ |
| Screw feed | $p$, $\omega$, $t$ | $L$ | $L = p \cdot \omega \cdot t / 60$ |
| Interference | Two part volumes $V_A$, $V_B$ | Overlap volume | $V_{int} = V_A \cap V_B$ |
| Clearance fit | $D_{hole}$, $D_{shaft}$ | Clearance $C$ | $C = D_{hole} - D_{shaft}$ |
| Tolerance stack | Stack of $n$ tolerances | Total tolerance | $T_{total} = \sqrt{\sum t_i^2}$ (RSS) or $\sum t_i$ (worst case) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Assembly Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Assembly Task] --> B0[Step 1: Analyze Real Machine]
    B0 --> C0[Step 2: Identify Components]
    C0 --> D0[Step 3: Model Each Part Individually]
    D0 --> E0[Step 4: Create New Assembly File]
    E0 --> F0[Step 5: Import First Part as Fixed]
    F0 --> G0[Step 6: Import Second Part]
    G0 --> H0[Step 7: Apply Mates Sequentially]
    H0 --> I0[Step 8: Verify Constraint Status]
    I0 --> J0{Constrained Correctly?}
    J0 -- No --> H0
    J0 -- Yes --> K0[Step 9: Perform Interference Check]
    K0 --> L0{Interference Found?}
    L0 -- Yes --> M0[Adjust Geometry or Mates]
    M0 --> H0
    L0 -- No --> N0[Step 10: Generate Exploded View]
    N0 --> O0[Step 11: Generate BOM]
    O0 --> P0[Step 12: Render and Document]
    P0 --> Q0[End: Final Assembly Delivered]
```

## 4.2 Mate Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Choose Mate Type] --> S1{What surfaces are involved?}
    S1 -- Two flat faces --> S2{Should they touch?}
    S1 -- Two cylindrical faces --> S3{Should axes align?}
    S1 -- Flat face and cylinder --> S4{Should face lie on axis end?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S5[Coincident Mate]
    S2 -- No, maintain gap --> S6[Distance Mate]
    S3 -- Yes --> S7[Concentric Mate]
    S3 -- No, parallel offset --> S8[Parallel Mate]
    S4 -- Yes --> S9[Coincident Mate]
    S4 -- No, tangent --> S10[Tangent Mate]
    S5 --> END1[Applied]
    S6 --> END1
    S7 --> END1
    S8 --> END1
    S9 --> END1
    S10 --> END1
```

## 4.3 Assembly Hierarchy Tree (Example: Simple Gearbox)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Top Assembly: Gearbox] --> P1[Part 1: Housing]
    ROOT --> P2[Part 2: Input Shaft]
    ROOT --> P3[Part 3: Output Shaft]
    ROOT --> SUB1[Sub-Assembly 1: Bearing Set]
    ROOT --> SUB2[Sub-Assembly 2: Gear Pair]
    SUB1 --> P4[Part 4: Bearing A]
    SUB1 --> P5[Part 5: Bearing B]
    SUB2 --> P6[Part 6: Pinion]
    SUB2 --> P7[Part 7: Gear]
    SUB2 --> P8[Part 8: Key]
```

## 4.4 Mate Type vs. DOF Removal Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph START[Initial State of a Single Part]
        A0[6 DOF: 3 Translations + 3 Rotations]
    end
    subgraph COINC[Coincident Mate]
        A1[Removes: 1 Translation + 1 Rotation = 2 DOF]
    end
    subgraph CONC[Concentric Mate]
        A2[Removes: 2 Translations = 2 DOF]
    end
    subgraph LOCK[Lock Mate]
        A3[Removes: 3 Translations + 3 Rotations = 6 DOF - fixes part]
    end
    A0 --> COINC
    A0 --> CONC
    A0 --> LOCK
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Assembly" in the context of CAD. List any four types of geometric mates used in assembly modeling.**

**Model Answer:**

An **assembly** in CAD is a 3D digital model that combines multiple individual part files into a single functional unit by establishing spatial and mechanical relationships between them using geometric constraints called **mates**.

**Four types of mates:**

1. **Coincident Mate** – Places two faces in the same plane (flange contact).
2. **Concentric Mate** – Aligns the axes of two cylindrical or conical features (shaft in a hole).
3. **Distance Mate** – Maintains a specified gap between two selected faces (spring preload, shim).
4. **Tangent Mate** – Makes two surfaces touch at exactly one point (belt on pulley, cam-follower contact).

> *(Examiner note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing mates with one-line use. No diagrams needed for 3-mark questions, but a small sketch earns goodwill.)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is a Bill of Materials (BOM)? Mention any four columns typically present in a BOM table.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is a structured tabular document that lists every component, sub-assembly, and raw material required to manufacture the final assembled product. It is automatically generated by the CAD assembly file and serves as the master reference for procurement, manufacturing, and quality control.

**Four typical columns of a BOM:**

1. **Item Number** – Sequential identifier (1, 2, 3 …).
2. **Part Number / Part ID** – Unique alphanumeric code for the component.
3. **Description** – Human-readable name of the part (e.g., "Output Shaft").
4. **Quantity** – Number of instances of that part used in one assembly.
5. *(Additional valid column: Material, Mass, Supplier, etc.)*

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> Internal Choice: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the step-by-step procedure for creating a CAD assembly of machine elements using the **bottom-up approach**. List the steps in logical order.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An assembly has **4 movable parts** and the following mates applied:

- 2 Concentric mates (each removes 2 DOF)
- 3 Coincident mates (each removes 2 DOF)
- 1 Distance mate (removes 1 DOF)

Determine whether the assembly is **fully constrained, under-constrained, or over-constrained**. Justify with calculation.

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Procedure for Bottom-Up CAD Assembly:**

1. **Analyze the Real Machine:** Disassemble the machine (mentally or physically) and identify all distinct parts.
2. **Plan the Assembly Sequence:** Decide the order in which parts will be added (typically, housing or frame first as the fixed base).
3. **Model Each Part Individually:** Use part-mode CAD to create each component, save each as a separate file (`.sldprt`, `.ipt`, `.prt`).
4. **Create a New Assembly File:** Open the assembly module and save with a suitable name.
5. **Insert the First Part (Fixed):** Import the base/housing and fix it in space (ground/anchor).
6. **Insert Subsequent Parts:** Import one part at a time into the assembly workspace.
7. **Apply Mates:** For each new part, select the relevant faces/edges/axes and apply appropriate mate types.
8. **Verify Constraint Status:** Check the CAD tree — all mates should appear in **black** (valid) not red/yellow (error).
9. **Check for Interference:** Run a collision/interference tool to confirm no parts overlap.
10. **Generate Exploded View & BOM:** Create an exploded view for documentation and generate the Bill of Materials.

*(A block diagram should accompany this — see Section 4.1.)*

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $n = 4$ movable parts
- 2 Concentric mates, $c = 2$ each → total $2 \times 2 = 4$ DOF removed
- 3 Coincident mates, $c = 2$ each → total $3 \times 2 = 6$ DOF removed
- 1 Distance mate, $c = 1$ → total $1 \times 1 = 1$ DOF removed

**Step 1 — Calculate total DOF of the unconstrained assembly:**

$$DOF_{total} = 6n = 6 \times 4 = 24$$

**Step 2 — Calculate total DOF removed by all mates:**

$$\sum c_i = 4 + 6 + 1 = 11$$

**Step 3 — Calculate residual DOF:**

$$n_{DOF} = 6n - \sum c_i = 24 - 11 = 13$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result:**

Since $n_{DOF} = 13 > 0$, the assembly is **UNDER-CONSTRAINED**.

> The assembly has 13 residual degrees of freedom — parts will float or jiggle when the assembly is moved. To make it fully constrained, **13 more DOF** must be removed through additional mates (or stronger mates that remove more DOF, e.g., a Lock mate that removes 6).

**Justification Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|:-----:|
| Required DOF removal for static assembly | 24 |
| Actual DOF removal | 11 |
| Shortfall | 13 |
| **Conclusion** | **Under-constrained** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Bottom-Up** and **Top-Down** assembly design strategies. State two advantages and one limitation of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pinion with **20 teeth** drives a gear with **60 teeth** at **1500 rpm**. Calculate:
   (i) The speed of the driven gear.
   (ii) The velocity ratio.
   (iii) The center-to-center distance if the module is **2 mm**.

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

| Aspect | Bottom-Up Assembly | Top-Down Assembly |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Order of modeling** | Parts first, assembly later | Skeleton/layout first, parts derived from it |
| **Reference** | Parts reference each other through mates | Parts reference the central skeleton |
| **Flexibility** | High — parts reusable across assemblies | Lower — geometry tightly coupled to skeleton |
| **Ease for KTU lab** | Easier, recommended for beginners | Requires more experience |
| **Design change impact** | Manual propagation needed | Automatic propagation through skeleton |

**Bottom-Up — Advantages:**
1. Simple to learn; ideal for students and small assemblies.
2. Parts are independent and can be reused in multiple assemblies without modification.

**Bottom-Up — Limitation:**
1. Design changes in a base part may require manual updates in all dependent assemblies.

**Top-Down — Advantages:**
1. A single change in the skeleton automatically updates all derived parts — excellent for design families.
2. Ensures that all parts share consistent key dimensions and interfaces from the start.

**Top-Down — Limitation:**
1. More complex to set up; requires careful skeleton planning, otherwise the entire assembly may need rework.

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $N_1 = 20$ teeth (pinion, driver)
- $N_2 = 60$ teeth (gear, driven)
- $n_1 = 1500$ rpm
- $m = 2$ mm (module)

**(i) Speed of driven gear:**

Using the fundamental relation $\omega_1 \cdot N_1 = \omega_2 \cdot N_2$:

$$n_2 = \frac{N_1}{N_2} \cdot n_1 = \frac{20}{60} \times 1500$$

$$n_2 = \frac{1}{3} \times 1500 = 500 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Velocity ratio:**

$$i = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{60}{20} = 3$$

This is a **speed reduction** of 3:1.

**(iii) Center-to-center distance:**

Pitch radius of pinion: $\quad r_1 = \dfrac{m \cdot N_1}{2} = \dfrac{2 \times 20}{2} = 20$ mm

Pitch radius of gear: $\quad r_2 = \dfrac{m \cdot N_2}{2} = \dfrac{2 \times 60}{2} = 60$ mm

Center distance:

$$a = r_1 + r_2 = 20 + 60 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **DOF Calculation Error (–2 to –3 marks):** Many students forget the factor of **6** (rigid body DOF) and write $n_{DOF} = n - \sum c_i$. This is **wrong**. Always start with $6n$, then subtract removed DOF.
> 2. **Mixing Up Bottom-Up and Top-Down (–2 marks):** If the question says "skeleton-driven," that is **Top-Down**, not Bottom-Up. Don't write definitions in reverse.
> 3. **Missing BOM in Lab Report (–2 marks in continuous evaluation):** Any assembly experiment without a BOM is considered incomplete.
> 4. **Not Showing Units:** Numerical answers without **mm**, **rpm**, **mm³** etc. lose half a mark each. Always include units.
> 5. **Exploded View Without Arrows:** In a proper exploded view, each part's trajectory should be indicated by a straight dashed/solid line (the "explode line") along the disassembly direction.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An **assembly** combines multiple **parts** via **mates** to simulate real machine behavior.
- ✅ **Bottom-up** = parts first → assembly; **Top-down** = skeleton first → parts derived.
- ✅ A rigid body in 3D has **6 DOF**; mates remove 1–6 DOF each.
- ✅ **Fully constrained** static assembly: $\;6n - \sum c_i = 0$.
- ✅ **Under-constrained:** $\;6n - \sum c_i > 0$ (parts float — bad).
- ✅ **Over-constrained:** $\;6n - \sum c_i < 0$ (redundant mates — bad).
- ✅ **Interference check** ensures $V_{int} = V(A \cap B) = 0$.
- ✅ **BOM** must include Item No., Part No., Description, Material, Quantity.
- ✅ **Gear mate ratio:** $\;\omega_1 N_1 = \omega_2 N_2$.
- ✅ **Screw mate linear travel:** $\;L = p \cdot \omega \cdot t / 60$.
- ✅ **Center distance of two meshing gears:** $\;a = m(N_1 + N_2)/2$.
- ✅ Always **fix the first part** (ground/anchor) before adding mates.
- ✅ Always **run an interference check** before final submission.
- ✅ Always include an **exploded view** and **BOM** in your KTU lab report.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3D Printing of Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL407 - COMPUTER AIDED MODELING AND SIMULATION OF MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 3D Printing of Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3D Printing (Additive Manufacturing, AM)** is defined by ASTM F2792 as the *process of joining materials to make objects from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing methodologies such as traditional machining.*

In the context of **PCCPL407 – Computer Aided Modeling and Simulation of Mechanical Systems**, *Module Experiment 2* requires the student to:
1. **Design** a 3D solid model of a mechanical component in any parametric CAD package (e.g., SolidWorks, CATIA, Fusion 360, Creo, NX).
2. **Export** the model into a neutral mesh format (industry standard: **.STL – STereoLithography**).
3. **Slice** the STL using a slicer software (e.g., **Cura**, PrusaSlicer, Simplify3D) to generate **G-code**.
4. **Transmit** the G-code to an **FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling)** or **SLA (Stereolithography)** printer.
5. **Fabricate** the object and perform any required post-processing (support removal, sanding, curing).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Inkjet Cake"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a normal inkjet printer that prints on paper, but instead of ink, it squeezes out melted plastic, and instead of paper, the paper is a flat platform. The platform drops down a microscopic amount, and the printer prints the next layer on top of the previous one — building a solid object from the bottom up, one flat slice at a time.**

That is exactly what 3D printing does:
- The **paper** = the build platform.
- The **ink** = molten thermoplastic filament (FDM) or photo-polymer resin (SLA).
- The **2D pages** = individual cross-sectional layers sliced from your CAD model.
- The **finished document** = a fully formed, three-dimensional mechanical part.

## 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in a B.Tech Mechanical Curriculum

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Computer-aided modeling is incomplete without a physical realization loop. Additive Manufacturing closes the **CAD → CAM → Physical Prototype** cycle, allowing engineers to validate FEA simulations, tolerance stacks, and ergonomic fit on a tangible artifact within hours instead of weeks.

Key physical / process constants the student must remember:

- **Standard STL tolerance (chordal deviation):** **0.01 mm** (default) — but coarser values (0.1–0.5 mm) are accepted for visual prototypes.
- **Common FDM layer heights:** **0.1 mm (fine)** to **0.3 mm (draft)**.
- **Nozzle diameter (typical FDM):** **0.4 mm**.
- **Glass transition temperature of PLA:** **≈ 60 °C**.
- **Glass transition temperature of ABS:** **≈ 105 °C**.
- **Print bed temperature range:** **50–110 °C** depending on filament.
- **Standard build envelope of a lab-grade FDM printer:** **200 × 200 × 200 mm** to **300 × 300 × 300 mm**.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 3D Printing Workflow (The 8-Stage Pipeline)

1. **Conceptualization** – Define the functional requirement (load-bearing bracket, jigsaw puzzle part, spur gear, robotic gripper finger, etc.).
2. **CAD Authoring** – Model the part using a parametric/sketch-based tool. Apply design-for-additive-manufacturing (DFAM) rules: minimum wall thickness ≥ **1.2 × nozzle diameter**, avoid large flat overhangs > **45°**, add fillets to internal corners.
3. **File Export — STL Generation** – The CAD kernel tessellates the B-rep (Boundary Representation) surface into a triangular mesh. Each triangle is defined by 3 vertices and a unit normal vector.
4. **Mesh Repair** – Slicer software checks for **non-manifold edges**, **inverted normals**, **intersecting triangles**, and **holes**. Tools: **Meshmixer**, **Netfabb**, **Blender (3D Print Toolbox)**.
5. **Slicing (Slicer Operation)** – The slicer slices the mesh into horizontal layers of user-defined thickness **h_l** and generates **G-code** (`.gcode` file), which is essentially a list of linear moves (`G0`/`G1`), extruder commands (`E`), and temperature setpoints (`M104`/`M140`).
6. **Print Job Setup** – Filament loaded, bed levelled, first-layer squish verified.
7. **Additive Fabrication** – Layer-by-layer material deposition.
8. **Post-Processing** – Support removal, sanding, bead-blasting, acetone vapor smoothing (ABS), UV curing (resin).

## 2.2 Key Process Parameters (KTU Frequently Tested)

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on Part |
|---|-----------|--------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | Layer Height | $h_l$ | 0.05–0.3 mm | Smaller = better resolution, longer print time |
| 2 | Nozzle Diameter | $d_n$ | 0.2–0.8 mm | Smaller = finer detail |
| 3 | Print Speed | $v_p$ | 30–150 mm/s | Higher = faster, risk of vibration |
| 4 | Infill Density | $\rho_i$ | 10–100 % | Higher = stronger, heavier |
| 5 | Infill Pattern | — | Grid, Triangular, Honeycomb, Gyroid | Affects strength-to-weight ratio |
| 6 | Shell/Perimeter Count | $n_s$ | 2–5 | Outer walls, affects surface finish |
| 7 | Top/Bottom Layer Count | $n_{tb}$ | 3–6 | Sealing of infill |
| 8 | Nozzle Temperature | $T_n$ | 180–250 °C | Material-dependent |
| 9 | Bed Temperature | $T_b$ | 50–110 °C | Adhesion, warping control |
| 10 | Print Orientation | $\theta$ | 0–90° | Strength is highly anisotropic |

## 2.3 The 7 Families of Additive Manufacturing (ASTM F2792)

1. **Vat Photopolymerization** – SLA, DLP (Stereolithography / Digital Light Processing)
2. **Material Extrusion** – FDM / FFF (Fused Deposition Modeling / Fused Filament Fabrication)
3. **Material Jetting** – PolyJet
4. **Binder Jetting** – Plaster / metal binder
5. **Powder Bed Fusion** – SLS (polymer), SLM / DMLS (metal)
6. **Directed Energy Deposition** – Laser cladding
7. **Sheet Lamination** – LOM (Laminated Object Manufacturing)

## 2.4 High-Yield KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear frequently in KTU numericals, viva, and lab-record viva-voce.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | STL File Size (rough) | $S_{STL} \approx 50 \cdot N_t$ bytes | bytes | $N_t$ = number of triangles |
| 2 | Build Time Estimate | $t_b = \dfrac{N_l}{v_p} \cdot k_f$ | seconds | $N_l$ = total line length; $k_f$ = acceleration factor (1.2–1.5) |
| 3 | Number of Layers | $N_l = \left\lceil \dfrac{H}{h_l} \right\rceil$ | layers | $H$ = part height in Z |
| 4 | Theoretical Filament Length | $L_f = \dfrac{V_p \cdot \rho_{rel}}{\pi r_f^2}$ | mm | $V_p$ = part volume, $r_f$ = filament radius (1.75 mm), $\rho_{rel}$ = infill ratio |
| 5 | Filament Mass | $m_f = L_f \cdot \rho_{fil} \cdot A_{fil}$ | grams | $A_{fil} = \pi r_f^2$ |
| 6 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q_v = v_p \cdot A_{ex} = v_p \cdot \pi (d_n/2)^2$ | mm³/s | Must stay below printer's max extrusion |
| 7 | Surface Roughness (FDM) | $R_a \approx \dfrac{h_l}{2}$ | mm | Empirical — half of layer height |
| 8 | Anisotropic Strength Ratio | $k_{xy/z} \approx 0.4$ to $0.8$ | dimensionless | Z (vertical) is always weaker |
| 9 | Resin Curing Energy (SLA) | $E_c = I \cdot t_{cure}$ | mJ/cm² | $I$ = UV intensity, $t_{cure}$ = exposure time |
| 10 | STL Resolution (chordal) | $C_{err} = R \left(1 - \cos\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ | mm | $R$ = CAD face radius, $\theta$ = chordal angle |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pitfall Avoided:** In all formulas above I have used `\vert`, `\lceil`, `\rceil` style notation, and curly braces — **never the raw pipe `|` character** — to prevent breaking the markdown table parser.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rapid Prototyping** – Functional testing of gears, brackets, and snap-fits.
- **Topology Optimization Validation** – Print organic, lattice-optimized structures impossible by machining.
- **Biomedical** – Patient-specific implants, surgical guides, prosthetics.
- **Aerospace** – Lightweight bionic lattices (GE LEAP engine fuel nozzle).
- **Automotive** – Jigs, fixtures, low-volume end-use parts.
- **Tooling** – Investment casting patterns.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Estimating Build Time for an FDM Print

**Problem Statement:** A rectangular block of dimensions **80 mm × 60 mm × 40 mm** is to be printed in **PLA** on an FDM printer with the following parameters:
- Layer height: $h_l = 0.2$ mm
- Print speed: $v_p = 60$ mm/s
- Acceleration penalty factor: $k_f = 1.3$
- 3 perimeters, **20 % infill**, gyroid pattern.
- Filament diameter: $d_f = 1.75$ mm; density: $\rho_{PLA} = 1.24$ g/cm³.

**Find:** (i) Number of layers, (ii) Approximate build time, (iii) Filament mass consumed.

### Step 1 — Number of Layers

The build direction is Z. Using the formula from the cheat sheet:

$$
N_l = \left\lceil \frac{H}{h_l} \right\rceil
$$

Substitute $H = 40$ mm and $h_l = 0.2$ mm:

$$
N_l = \left\lceil \frac{40}{0.2} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 200 \right\rceil = 200 \text{ layers}
$$

> **Logic:** The ceiling function is mandatory because the last layer is never a partial layer — the slicer rounds up to the nearest whole layer.

### Step 2 — Build Time

For each layer, the printhead traces a perimeter path and infill. We approximate the **perimeter length per layer** as the perimeter of one outer rectangle, plus two inner offsets (since there are 3 perimeters, the offsets are $0.4, 0.8$ mm inward). Approximating perimeter per layer:

$$
P_{layer} = 2(L + W) \approx 2(80 + 60) = 280 \text{ mm}
$$

With 3 perimeters, the perimeter length per layer is $3 \times 280 = 840$ mm. Plus infill contribution (20 %), let us assume the **total extruded path per layer** $L_{path} \approx 1200$ mm.

Apply the time formula:

$$
t_{layer} = \frac{L_{path} \cdot k_f}{v_p} = \frac{1200 \times 1.3}{60} = 26 \text{ s/layer}
$$

Total build time:

$$
t_{total} = N_l \cdot t_{layer} = 200 \times 26 = 5200 \text{ s}
$$

Converting:

$$
t_{total} = \frac{5200}{60} \approx 86.7 \text{ min} \approx 1 \text{ h } 27 \text{ min}
$$

### Step 3 — Filament Mass

Part volume:

$$
V_p = L \cdot W \cdot H = 80 \times 60 \times 40 = 192000 \text{ mm}^3 = 192 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Effective material volume (accounting for 20 % infill + 3 perimeters, let effective fill ratio $\rho_{rel} \approx 0.35$):

$$
V_{eff} = 0.35 \times 192 = 67.2 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Filament cross-section area:

$$
A_{fil} = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.75^2}{4} \approx 2.405 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.02405 \text{ cm}^2
$$

Filament length required:

$$
L_f = \frac{V_{eff}}{A_{fil}} = \frac{67.2}{0.02405} \approx 2794 \text{ cm} = 27.94 \text{ m}
$$

Filament mass:

$$
m_f = V_{eff} \times \rho_{PLA} = 67.2 \times 1.24 \approx 83.3 \text{ g}
$$

> **Sanity Check:** A 1 kg spool of PLA is ~330 m of 1.75 mm filament. Our 28 m draw is ~2.8 % of a spool, which is realistic for a small bracket.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — STL File Size and Triangle Count

**Problem Statement:** A sphere of diameter **50 mm** is exported with a chordal deviation of **0.1 mm**. Estimate the STL file size.

### Step 1 — Relate chordal deviation to triangle face count

The surface area of a sphere of radius $R = 25$ mm:

$$
A_{sphere} = 4 \pi R^2 = 4 \pi (25)^2 = 2500 \pi \approx 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2
$$

For a chordal deviation $C_{err} = 0.1$ mm on a curved surface of radius $R$, the average edge length of a triangle is approximately:

$$
\ell \approx 2 \sqrt{2 R \cdot C_{err}} = 2 \sqrt{2 \times 25 \times 0.1} = 2 \sqrt{5} \approx 4.472 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Number of Triangles

For an equilateral triangle of side $\ell$, the area is:

$$
A_{tri} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \ell^2
$$

Substituting:

$$
A_{tri} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} (4.472)^2 \approx \frac{1.732}{4} \times 20 = 8.66 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Number of triangles needed:

$$
N_t = \left\lceil \frac{A_{sphere}}{A_{tri}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{7853.98}{8.66} \right\rceil \approx 907 \text{ triangles}
$$

### Step 3 — STL File Size

Each triangle in ASCII STL requires exactly **50 bytes** (3 vertices × 12 bytes + normal vector 12 bytes + attribute 2 bytes + keyword overhead). For binary STL, the overhead is 80 bytes header + 4 bytes count + 50 bytes/triangle:

$$
S_{STL,binary} = 84 + 50 \cdot N_t = 84 + 50 \times 907 \approx 45484 \text{ bytes} \approx 44.4 \text{ kB}
$$

ASCII STL would be ~6× larger (~265 kB).

> **Engineering Insight:** Slicers like **Cura** recommend binary STL because it is ~6× smaller and 10× faster to parse. For complex organic shapes (lattices, topology-optimized brackets), the triangle count can exceed 1 million, making ASCII STL prohibitive.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Volumetric Flow Rate Limit

**Problem Statement:** A 0.4 mm nozzle prints at 80 mm/s. Calculate the volumetric flow rate and verify it does not exceed a typical hot-end limit of 12 mm³/s.

$$
Q_v = v_p \cdot A_{nozzle} = v_p \cdot \frac{\pi d_n^2}{4}
$$

$$
Q_v = 80 \times \frac{\pi (0.4)^2}{4} = 80 \times 0.1257 = 10.05 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

Since $10.05 < 12$, the print is **safe**. If we tried 150 mm/s, $Q_v$ would be 18.85 mm³/s, which would cause **extruder skipping** and **under-extrusion**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right 3D Printing Process

| Selection Criterion | FDM (Material Extrusion) | SLA (Vat Photopolymerization) | SLS (Powder Bed Fusion) |
|---------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Lab Cost (₹)** | 15,000 – 80,000 | 25,000 – 1,50,000 | 5,00,000+ |
| **Material Cost/kg** | ₹800 – 2,500 (PLA/ABS) | ₹3,000 – 8,000 (Resin) | ₹5,000 – 15,000 (Nylon) |
| **Layer Height Range** | 0.05 – 0.3 mm | 0.02 – 0.1 mm | 0.06 – 0.15 mm |
| **Surface Finish** | Visible layer lines | Smooth, glossy | Grainy, matte |
| **Tolerance** | ±0.2 mm | ±0.05 mm | ±0.1 mm |
| **Best For (B.Tech Lab)** | Brackets, gears, jigs | Flow visualization, dental | Functional nylon parts |
| **Strength Direction** | Highly anisotropic | Moderate | Nearly isotropic |
| **Post-Processing** | Easy (sand/file) | UV cure + alcohol wash | Bead-blast + dye |

> **KTU Tip:** For Module Experiment 2, the **most commonly available printer in Kerala engineering colleges is FDM** (e.g., Creality Ender 3, Prusa i3, Wanhao). Plan your experiment around FDM.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete 3D Printing Workflow (Process Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Part Function] --> B[Step 2: CAD Modeling in SolidWorks / Fusion 360]
    B --> C[Step 3: Apply DFAM Rules Wall thickness, fillets, overhangs]
    C --> D[Step 4: Export to STL Binary format preferred]
    D --> E[Step 5: Mesh Repair in Meshmixer Manifold check, normals]
    E --> F[Step 6: Import to Slicer Cura / PrusaSlicer]
    F --> G[Step 7: Set Print Parameters Layer height, infill, supports]
    G --> H[Step 8: Slice and Generate G-code]
    H --> I[Step 9: Load Filament and Level Bed]
    I --> J[Step 10: Start Print Job]
    J --> K[Step 11: Monitor First Layer Adhesion]
    K --> L[Step 12: Post Processing Support removal, sanding, curing]
    L --> M[Step 13: Final Inspection and Measurement]
    M --> N[Step 14: Compare with CAD Model Tolerance check]
```

## 4.2 FDM Printer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph XAxis[X AXIS ASSEMBLY]
        X1[Stepper Motor X] --> X2[Belt Drive] --> X3[Printhead Carriage]
    end
    subgraph YAxis[Y AXIS ASSEMBLY]
        Y1[Y Stepper] --> Y2[Y Belt] --> Y3[Heated Bed Platform]
    end
    subgraph ZAxis[Z AXIS ASSEMBLY]
        Z1[Z Lead Screw] --> Z2[Z Coupler] --> Z3[Z Frame]
    end
    subgraph HotEnd[HOT END ASSEMBLY]
        H1[Extruder Gear] --> H2[Bowden Tube] --> H3[Heater Block] --> H4[Brass Nozzle 0.4 mm]
    end
    subgraph Control[CONTROL BOARD]
        C1[Arduino / 32-bit Board] --> C2[Stepper Drivers] --> C3[Heater MOSFETs] --> C4[Endstops and Thermistors]
    end
    XAxis --> Control
    YAxis --> Control
    ZAxis --> Control
    HotEnd --> Control
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic — Support Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Part Loaded in Slicer] --> Q1{Overhang Angle less than 45 degrees}
    Q1 -- Yes --> NoSup[No Supports Needed Print Directly]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Bridge Distance less than 10 mm}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Bridging[Bridging Mode Printhead moves fast]
    Q2 -- No --> Sup[Generate Tree or Linear Supports]
    Sup --> Orient{Quieter option: Re orient Part in CAD}
    Orient -- Yes --> NewOrient[Rotate Model in CAD and Re slice]
    Orient -- No --> Print[Proceed with Supports]
    NoSup --> Print
    Bridging --> Print
    NewOrient --> Print
```

## 4.4 G-Code Anatomy (What the Slicer Outputs)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Header G-code] --> B[Machine Init G28 Home All Axes]
    B --> C[Bed Preheat M140 S60]
    C --> D[Nozzle Preheat M104 S210]
    D --> E[Layer 1 G1 Z0.2]
    E --> F[Print Perimeter G1 X10 Y10 E0.5]
    F --> G[Print Infill G1 X20 Y20 E1.2]
    G --> H[Layer 2 G1 Z0.4]
    H --> I[Repeat Until Z equals Part Height]
    I --> J[End G-code M104 S0 and M140 S0]
```

## 4.5 Comparison Block Diagram — 3 AM Technologies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AM[Additive Manufacturing ASTM F2792] --> P1[Material Extrusion FDM]
    AM --> P2[Vat Photopolymerization SLA]
    AM --> P3[Powder Bed Fusion SLS and SLM]
    P1 --> M1[Filament 1.75 mm Thermoplastic PLA ABS PETG]
    P2 --> M2[Liquid Photopolymer Resin UV Curable]
    P3 --> M3[Powder Nylon 12 Stainless Steel 316L]
    M1 --> App1[Prototyping Jigs Educational]
    M2 --> App2[Dental Models Microfluidics Aesthetic]
    M3 --> App3[Functional Parts Aerospace Implants]
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**What is Additive Manufacturing? List any two ASTM F2792 process categories.**

**Model Answer:**
Additive Manufacturing (AM) is the process of joining materials to make objects from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing (ASTM F2792).

Two process categories:
1. **Material Extrusion** (e.g., FDM)
2. **Vat Photopolymerization** (e.g., SLA)

(Any other two: Material Jetting, Binder Jetting, Powder Bed Fusion, Directed Energy Deposition, Sheet Lamination – full credit.)

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**What is the difference between ASCII and Binary STL file formats? Which is preferred in industrial slicing?**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | ASCII STL | Binary STL |
|----------|-----------|------------|
| File size | ~6× larger | Compact |
| Human-readable | Yes (text) | No |
| Parse speed | Slow | 10× faster |
| Default in modern slicers | No | **Yes** |

**Industrial slicers (Cura, PrusaSlicer, Simplify3D) prefer Binary STL** because of smaller file size, faster parsing, and lower memory consumption. ASCII STL is only used for debugging and educational visualization.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete workflow of 3D printing a mechanical component**, starting from CAD modeling to post-processing. Mention the role of the slicer and G-code. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical pin of diameter **30 mm** and height **50 mm** is to be printed in ABS on an FDM machine. Given:
- Layer height: $h_l = 0.25$ mm
- Print speed: $v_p = 50$ mm/s
- Acceleration factor: $k_f = 1.25$
- 3 perimeters, **30 %** infill
- Filament: ABS, diameter **1.75 mm**, density **1.04 g/cm³**
- Effective volumetric fill ratio: $\rho_{rel} = 0.42$

Calculate: (i) Number of layers, (ii) Filament length required, (iii) Filament mass, (iv) Approximate build time assuming 600 mm of extruded path per layer. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The workflow consists of 8 stages as illustrated in the block diagram:

1. **CAD Authoring** – A 3D solid model is created using parametric software. DFAM rules are applied: minimum wall thickness ≥ 1.2 × nozzle diameter, fillets on internal corners, avoidance of large overhangs > 45°.
2. **STL Export** – The B-rep surface is tessellated into a triangular mesh. Binary STL is preferred.
3. **Mesh Repair** – Slicer/Netfabb checks for non-manifold edges, holes, and inverted normals.
4. **Slicing** – The slicer (Cura) slices the mesh into horizontal layers of thickness $h_l$ and computes toolpaths. It also generates support structures for overhangs.
5. **G-code Generation** – A text file (`.gcode`) is produced containing movement (`G0`, `G1`), extrusion (`E`), and temperature (`M104`, `M140`) commands. **G-code is the universal machine language of CNC/3D printers.**
6. **Print Setup** – Filament loaded, bed levelled, Z-offset calibrated.
7. **Additive Fabrication** – Material is deposited layer-by-layer.
8. **Post-Processing** – Support removal, sanding, acetone vapor smoothing (for ABS), UV curing (for resin).

> [!NOTE]
> **Block diagram (text version for answer sheet):**
> `CAD Model → STL Export → Mesh Repair → Slicer (Layer Decomposition) → G-code → FDM Printer → Layer Deposition → Post-Processing → Final Part`

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Number of layers:**

$$
N_l = \left\lceil \frac{H}{h_l} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{50}{0.25} \right\rceil = 200 \text{ layers}
$$

**(ii) Filament length:**

Part volume:

$$
V_p = \pi r^2 H = \pi (15)^2 \times 50 = 11250 \pi \approx 35342.9 \text{ mm}^3 = 35.34 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Effective volume:

$$
V_{eff} = \rho_{rel} \times V_p = 0.42 \times 35.34 = 14.84 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Filament cross-section:

$$
A_{fil} = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (1.75)^2}{4} \approx 2.405 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.02405 \text{ cm}^2
$$

Filament length:

$$
L_f = \frac{V_{eff}}{A_{fil}} = \frac{14.84}{0.02405} \approx 617 \text{ cm} = 6.17 \text{ m}
$$

**(iii) Filament mass:**

$$
m_f = V_{eff} \times \rho_{ABS} = 14.84 \times 1.04 \approx 15.4 \text{ g}
$$

**(iv) Approximate build time:**

Per-layer time:

$$
t_{layer} = \frac{L_{path} \cdot k_f}{v_p} = \frac{600 \times 1.25}{50} = 15 \text{ s/layer}
$$

Total time:

$$
t_{total} = 200 \times 15 = 3000 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ min}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]

**(a)** Compare **FDM, SLA, and SLS** 3D printing technologies with respect to **working principle, materials used, surface finish, accuracy, and typical applications**. Mention the advantages and limitations of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A topology-optimized aerospace bracket has been exported as an STL with **125,000 triangles**.
(i) Estimate the binary STL file size in kB.
(ii) If the slicer is set to a layer height of 0.15 mm and the part height is 78 mm, how many layers will be generated?
(iii) The part contains **1,250 g of overhangs > 60°**. The slicer generates tree supports consuming 320 g of additional filament. What is the **support-to-part material ratio** and what design modification would you suggest to reduce supports? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

| Property | FDM | SLA | SLS |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|
| **Working Principle** | Thermoplastic filament melted and extruded through heated nozzle | Liquid photopolymer resin cured by UV laser | Powder fused by CO₂ or fibre laser |
| **Materials** | PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU, Nylon | UV-curable resin (standard, tough, flexible, castable) | Nylon 12, Nylon 11, TPU, Polypropylene |
| **Surface Finish** | Visible layer lines, Ra ≈ $h_l/2$ | Smooth, glossy, near-injection-moulded | Grainy, matte, slightly sandy |
| **Dimensional Accuracy** | ±0.2 mm | ±0.05 mm (best) | ±0.1–0.2 mm |
| **Support Requirement** | Yes, mandatory for overhangs > 45° | Yes, but minimal — small contact points | **No supports needed** — unsintered powder self-supports |
| **Strength** | Highly anisotropic (Z weaker) | Brittle, weak in thin sections | Nearly isotropic |
| **Build Volume (Lab)** | 200–300 mm³ | 130–200 mm³ | 200–300 mm³ |
| **Cost (Lab printer)** | ₹15k–80k | ₹25k–1.5L | ₹5L+ |
| **Typical Applications** | Educational, jigs, functional prototypes, end-use parts | Dental, jewellery, microfluidics, optical prototypes | Functional nylon parts, hinges, lattice structures |
| **Advantages** | Cheap, robust, wide material range | Highest accuracy and finish | No supports, functional parts |
| **Limitations** | Layer lines, anisotropic | Brittle, resin messy, post-cure needed | Expensive, grainy surface, powder handling |

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Binary STL file size:**

$$
S_{STL} = 84 + 50 \cdot N_t = 84 + 50 \times 125000 = 6250084 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
S_{STL} \approx \frac{6250084}{1024} \approx 6103.6 \text{ kB} \approx 5.96 \text{ MB}
$$

**(ii) Number of layers:**

$$
N_l = \left\lceil \frac{H}{h_l} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{78}{0.15} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 520 \right\rceil = 520 \text{ layers}
$$

**(iii) Support-to-part material ratio:**

$$
R_{sp} = \frac{m_{support}}{m_{overhang}} = \frac{320}{1250} = 0.256 \text{ or } 25.6 \%
$$

**Design modification to reduce supports:**
1. **Re-orient the part** in CAD so that the overhangs face upward (rotate 180° about the longest axis). This converts overhangs into flat-top surfaces that do not need supports.
2. **Apply chamfers or fillets** to the overhanging regions to reduce the effective overhang angle below 45°.
3. **Split the part** into sub-assemblies that can be printed flat.
4. **Use a different slicing strategy** — "Tree supports" with a 35° threshold (already optimal, but manual tree placement can be tuned).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Wrong unit conversion in filament mass problems.** Density of PLA is **1.24 g/cm³**, NOT 1.24 g/mm³. Always convert volume to cm³ before multiplying by density.
> 2. **Forgetting the acceleration factor $k_f$.** Without it, your build-time answer will be 25–50 % too optimistic. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Using `G` instead of `g` for grams**, or using inconsistent units in STL file size (kB vs MB).
> 4. **Forgetting the ceiling function** in the number-of-layers formula. Always write $\lceil \rceil$.
> 5. **Confusing ASCII vs Binary STL sizes.** ASCII is ~6× larger — not 2×.
> 6. **Writing "Stereolithography" for FDM** — the examiner will mark it wrong. FDM is Material Extrusion, not Vat Photopolymerization.
> 7. **Not mentioning G-code in workflow questions** — G-code is the mandatory bridge between slicer and printer; expect a -2 mark penalty.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam / Lab Viva**

### **Core Definitions**
- 3D Printing = Additive Manufacturing = ASTM F2792 = layer-by-layer material joining.
- **STL** = STereoLithography file = tessellated triangular mesh of CAD model.
- **G-code** = the machine language generated by the slicer.
- **Slicer** = software that converts STL into G-code (Cura, PrusaSlicer).

### **Must-Know Numbers**
- 1 triangle in Binary STL = 50 bytes (plus 84 bytes header).
- ASCII STL is ~6× larger than Binary.
- FDM layer height range: **0.05 – 0.3 mm**.
- Standard lab nozzle: **0.4 mm**.
- Standard filament: **1.75 mm** diameter.
- PLA bed temp: **60 °C**; ABS bed temp: **100–110 °C**.
- Glass transition: PLA ≈ 60 °C; ABS ≈ 105 °C.
- Overhang threshold: **> 45°** requires supports (FDM).
- SLS does **not** require supports.

### **Must-Know Formulas**
$$
N_l = \left\lceil \frac{H}{h_l} \right\rceil, \quad
t_{total} = N_l \cdot \frac{L_{path} \cdot k_f}{v_p}, \quad
m_f = \frac{V_p \cdot \rho_{rel} \cdot \rho_{fil}}{A_{fil}}
$$

### **Process Categories (ASTM F2792) — Mnemonic: "Very Bad Men Build Pretty Dumb Structures"**
1. **V**at Photopolymerization
2. **B**inder Jetting
3. **M**aterial Extrusion
4. **B** (same — actually Powder Bed Fusion)
5. **P**owder Bed Fusion
6. **D**irected Energy Deposition
7. **S**heet Lamination

### **Lab Record Must-Haves for Module Experiment 2**
- Aim & Apparatus list.
- CAD model screenshots (isometric + orthographic).
- STL file exported with chordal deviation noted.
- Slicer screenshot showing layer preview, supports, infill.
- Print parameter table.
- Calculation of estimated time, filament mass.
- Photograph of the **first layer** during printing.
- Photograph of the **finished part** next to a ruler.
- Post-processing steps performed.
- Comparison of nominal vs measured dimensions (at least 3 features).
- Conclusion + applications of the part.

### **Common Lab Viva Questions**
1. *Why does the Z-direction of an FDM print have lower strength?*
   → Layer adhesion is weaker than intra-layer bonds due to partial re-melting.
2. *Why do we need a heated bed?*
   → To prevent warping by keeping the lower layers above glass-transition temperature.
3. *What happens if layer height is greater than nozzle diameter?*
   → The extruder cannot bridge the gap → under-extrusion and weak inter-layer bond.
4. *Why is binary STL preferred over ASCII?*
   → 6× smaller file size, 10× faster to parse, less memory.
5. *What is the role of `M104` and `M140` in G-code?*
   → M104 sets extruder temperature; M140 sets bed temperature.
6. *Name two CAD file formats that retain parametric history.*
   → STEP (`.step`), IGES (`.iges`), and native formats like `.sldprt`, `.f3d`, `.prt`.
7. *What is the difference between SLA and DLP?*
   → SLA uses a **point laser** to cure resin; DLP uses a **projector** to cure an entire layer at once — DLP is faster.
8. *Why are tree supports better than linear supports?*
   → Less material, easier to remove, less surface scarring on the part.

> **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: 3D Printing of Objects**
> *Prepared for KTU 2024 Scheme PCCPL407 — Computer Aided Modeling and Simulation of Mechanical Systems*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Simulation of Four Bar and Slider Crank Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL407 - COMPUTER AIDED MODELING AND SIMULATION OF MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Simulation of Four Bar and Slider Crank Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Kinematic Simulation

**Kinematic Simulation** is the computational (computer-aided) process of determining the geometric positions, velocities, and accelerations of the links of a mechanism as a function of time, *without* considering the forces that cause the motion. It is the cornerstone of mechanism design and is the first step before dynamic (force) analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Kinematic simulation involves the mathematical modeling of a mechanism's geometry using loop-closure equations or vector-loop methods, followed by their numerical solution (analytical matrix) to trace the motion of every link through a complete cycle.*

## 1.2 The Four-Bar Linkage

A **Four-Bar Mechanism** (also called a *quadric crank chain* or *four-bar chain*) is the most fundamental planar linkage consisting of **four rigid links** connected in a loop by **four revolute (pin) joints**.

**Components:**
- **Ground link (r₁)**: The fixed link of the frame
- **Input link / Crank (r₂)**: The driver link, which rotates fully (360°)
- **Coupler / Connecting rod (r₃)**: The intermediate link that does not connect to ground
- **Output link / Rocker (r₄)**: The driven link, which oscillates (less than 360°)

> [!NOTE]
> The *Grashof's Law* classifies it as: If **S + L ≤ P + Q** (where S = shortest, L = longest, P and Q = other two links), at least one link can rotate fully.

## 1.3 The Slider-Crank Mechanism

A **Slider-Crank Mechanism** is a four-bar chain in which one of the links (the fourth) is replaced by a **prismatic joint (slider)** that translates along a straight line. It is the heart of every internal combustion engine.

**Components:**
- **Crank (r)**: Rotating input link
- **Connecting rod (l)**: Coupler link
- **Slider (piston)**: Translating output
- **Guide / Cylinder line**: Defines the slider's straight-line path

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it this way...** 🛠️
>
> **Four-Bar Mechanism** → A **bicycle frame** viewed from the side. As you push the pedal (input crank), the connecting rod (chain) pushes the rear arm (rocker) back and forth. Your leg (input) moves in a circle, but the rear wheel swing oscillates.
>
> **Slider-Crank Mechanism** → A **sewing machine needle** or **car engine piston**. As the motor rotates the circular crank wheel, the connecting rod converts that rotation into an up-and-down (reciprocating) push of the needle/piston.
>
> **Kinematic Simulation** → It's like using a *flipbook animation* where each page is a precise mathematical frame of the mechanism at a tiny time step (Δt). The computer "draws" the next frame from the equations instead of by hand.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Angular velocity** $\omega$ → measured in **rad/s**
- **Angular acceleration** $\alpha$ → measured in **rad/s²**
- **Linear velocity** $v$ → measured in **m/s**
- **Linear acceleration** $a$ → measured in **m/s²**
- **Link length** $r$ or $l$ → measured in **m** or **mm**
- **Crank rotation** $\theta$ → measured in **degrees** or **radians**
- **Simulation time step** $\Delta t$ → typically **1° to 5°** of crank rotation

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Loop-Closure Equation (Vector Loop)

The mathematical backbone of any planar linkage simulation is the **vector loop equation**. It states that if you walk around the closed loop of links, the **sum of all position vectors must equal zero**.

### For a Four-Bar Mechanism:

**Step 1 — Vector Setup:**
Imagine the four vectors forming a closed polygon. If we start at the ground pivot O₂ and return to the same point:

$$\vec{r_2} + \vec{r_3} - \vec{r_4} - \vec{r_1} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Why this works:** The "Why" is geometry — if a closed chain returns to its origin, the sum of displacements around the loop is zero. This is the **fundamental kinematic constraint**.

**Step 3 — Decomposition into X and Y components:**

$$r_2 \cos\theta_2 + r_3 \cos\theta_3 = r_4 \cos\theta_4 + r_1 \cos\theta_1$$

$$r_2 \sin\theta_2 + r_3 \sin\theta_3 = r_4 \sin\theta_4 + r_1 \sin\theta_1$$

**Step 4 — Unknowns to Solve:** Given $\theta_2$ (input), find $\theta_3$ and $\theta_4$ (outputs). This is a **system of 2 non-linear equations in 2 unknowns**.

### For a Slider-Crank Mechanism:

**Vector loop** (with offset $d$ from crank center to slider axis):

$$\vec{r} + \vec{l} - \vec{x} = 0$$
