Where $\vec{x}$ is the slider position vector. Component form:

$$r \cos\theta + l \cos\phi = x$$

$$r \sin\theta - l \sin\phi = 0$$

## 2.2 The Analytical Matrix (Klein's Construction)

For a **generalized planar mechanism** with $n$ links, the position analysis can be written compactly in matrix form:

$$[A]\{\theta\} = \{B\}$$

Where:
- $[A]$ is the **coefficient matrix** built from link lengths and trigonometric functions
- $\{\theta\}$ is the **unknown joint displacement vector**
- $\{B\}$ is the **known input vector**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use a Matrix?** In CAE software like MATLAB or Python, this allows the same solver (e.g., Newton-Raphson) to be applied uniformly to ANY planar mechanism, regardless of its topology. This is the essence of **Computer-Aided Kinematic Simulation**.

## 2.3 Velocity Analysis (First Derivative)

Differentiate the position loop with respect to time:

**Four-Bar Velocity Equations:**

$$-r_2 \omega_2 \sin\theta_2 - r_3 \omega_3 \sin\theta_3 + r_4 \omega_4 \sin\theta_4 = 0$$

$$r_2 \omega_2 \cos\theta_2 + r_3 \omega_3 \cos\theta_3 - r_4 \omega_4 \cos\theta_4 = 0$$

Solve this **2×2 linear system** for $\omega_3$ and $\omega_4$ at each time step.

**Slider-Crank Velocity Equations:**

$$v_{slider} = -r\omega\sin\theta - l\omega_{rod}\sin\phi$$

$$\omega_{rod} = \frac{r\omega \cos\theta}{l \cos\phi}$$

## 2.4 Acceleration Analysis (Second Derivative)

Differentiate the velocity equations again:

**Four-Bar Acceleration Equations:**

$$-r_2 \alpha_2 \sin\theta_2 - r_2 \omega_2^2 \cos\theta_2 - r_3 \alpha_3 \sin\theta_3 - r_3 \omega_3^2 \cos\theta_3 + r_4 \alpha_4 \sin\theta_4 + r_4 \omega_4^2 \cos\theta_4 = 0$$

$$r_2 \alpha_2 \cos\theta_2 - r_2 \omega_2^2 \sin\theta_2 + r_3 \alpha_3 \cos\theta_3 - r_3 \omega_3^2 \sin\theta_3 - r_4 \alpha_4 \cos\theta_4 + r_4 \omega_4^2 \sin\theta_4 = 0$$

These are again **2 linear equations in 2 unknowns** ($\alpha_3$ and $\alpha_4$).

## 2.5 Freudenstein's Equation (Direct Position Solution)

A classic **closed-form** solution to the four-bar position problem:

$$K_1 \cos\theta_3 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3)$$

Where the dimensionless constants are:

$$K_1 = \frac{r_4}{r_2}, \quad K_2 = \frac{r_1}{r_2}, \quad K_3 = \frac{r_1^2 - r_2^2 + r_3^2 + r_4^2}{2 r_2 r_4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Freudenstein's equation important for KTU exams?** It eliminates the iterative Newton-Raphson solver and gives a direct trigonometric expression for $\theta_3$ given $\theta_2$ — perfect for hand calculation in board exams.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Mechanism Used | Why Kinematic Simulation? |
|---|---|---|
| **IC Engine Piston** | Slider-Crank | Piston displacement/velocity vs. crank angle is needed for combustion timing |
| **Washing Machine Agitator** | Four-Bar (Drag-link) | Optimizes stroke length without trial-and-error prototyping |
| **Wind Turbine Blade Pitch** | Four-Bar | Ensures smooth rotation over the full 360° |
| **Robotic Arm Joints** | Generalized Four-Bar | Path planning of end-effector |
| **Folding Furniture (Recliner)** | Four-Bar | Compute backrest tilt vs. footrest extension |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Position loop (4-bar) | $r_2\cos\theta_2 + r_3\cos\theta_3 = r_4\cos\theta_4 + r_1\cos\theta_1$ | $\theta_2$ input, $\theta_3,\theta_4$ unknown |
| 2 | Position loop (Slider-Crank) | $r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi = x$ | $x$ is slider displacement |
| 3 | Slider position | $x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$ | No offset case |
| 4 | Freudenstein constant $K_1$ | $K_1 = r_4 / r_2$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Freudenstein constant $K_2$ | $K_2 = r_1 / r_2$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Freudenstein constant $K_3$ | $K_3 = (r_1^2 - r_2^2 + r_3^2 + r_4^2) / (2 r_2 r_4)$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Freudenstein Equation | $K_1\cos\theta_3 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3)$ | Closed-form position solver |
| 8 | Velocity matrix (4-bar) | $[C]\{\omega\} = \{D\}$ | $2 \times 2$ linear system |
| 9 | Slider velocity | $\dot{x} = -r\omega\sin\theta \left(1 + \frac{r\cos\theta}{\sqrt{l^2-r^2\sin^2\theta}}\right)$ | Differentiate $x(\theta)$ |
| 10 | Slider acceleration | $\ddot{x} = -r\omega^2\cos\theta - r\alpha\sin\theta - \dot{\phi}^2 l\cos\phi - \alpha l\sin\phi$ | Full expansion |
| 11 | Grashof's Law | $S + L \le P + Q$ | For at least one full-revolving link |
| 12 | Coupler point velocity | $v_P = v_B + \omega_3 \times r_{BP}$ | Relative velocity method |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the Four-Bar Vector Loop:** *"**I-C-C-G**"* (Input–Coupler–Coupler–Ground). Walk: Ground → Input → Coupler → Output → Ground. The closing vector is the *ground link*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Four-Bar Loop-Closure Equation

**Given:** A planar four-bar mechanism with links of lengths $r_1, r_2, r_3, r_4$ and joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4$ measured from the global X-axis.

**Step 1 — Visualize the closed polygon.** Start at the ground pivot $O_2$, traverse the crank $r_2$ to point $A$, then the coupler $r_3$ to point $B$, then the rocker $r_4$ to ground pivot $O_4$. The fourth side is the ground link $r_1$ from $O_4$ back to $O_2$.

**Step 2 — Write the vector sum condition** (closed polygon = zero):

$$\vec{r_1} - \vec{r_2} - \vec{r_3} + \vec{r_4} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Express each vector in (X, Y) form** using magnitude-angle representation $\vec{r_i} = r_i(\cos\theta_i \,\hat{i} + \sin\theta_i \,\hat{j})$:

$$r_1\cos\theta_1 - r_2\cos\theta_2 - r_3\cos\theta_3 + r_4\cos\theta_4 = 0 \quad \text{(X-component)}$$

$$r_1\sin\theta_1 - r_2\sin\theta_2 - r_3\sin\theta_3 + r_4\sin\theta_4 = 0 \quad \text{(Y-component)}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate knowns and unknowns.** Known: $r_1, r_2, r_3, r_4, \theta_1, \theta_2$. Unknown: $\theta_3, \theta_4$.

$$r_3\cos\theta_3 - r_4\cos\theta_4 = r_1\cos\theta_1 - r_2\cos\theta_2$$

$$r_3\sin\theta_3 - r_4\sin\theta_4 = r_1\sin\theta_1 - r_2\sin\theta_2$$

**Step 5 — Square and add both equations** to eliminate the angle difference (this is the trick used in deriving Freudenstein's equation):

$$\begin{aligned}
r_3^2 + r_4^2 - 2 r_3 r_4 \cos(\theta_3 - \theta_4) &= (r_1\cos\theta_1 - r_2\cos\theta_2)^2 + (r_1\sin\theta_1 - r_2\sin\theta_2)^2 \\
&= r_1^2 + r_2^2 - 2 r_1 r_2 \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_1)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\cos(\theta_3 - \theta_4)$** explicitly:

$$\cos(\theta_3 - \theta_4) = \frac{r_1^2 + r_2^2 - r_3^2 - r_4^2 + 2 r_1 r_2 \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_1)}{2 r_3 r_4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This gives $\theta_4$ directly as a function of $\theta_2$! Then $\theta_3$ is found from a single linear equation by back-substitution. This is the **standard textbook analytical method** for four-bar position analysis.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Velocity Equations (Differentiation Method)

**Step 1 — Start with the X-component position equation:**

$$r_2\cos\theta_2 + r_3\cos\theta_3 = r_4\cos\theta_4 + r_1\cos\theta_1$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to time** (using $\dfrac{d}{dt}\cos\theta = -\omega\sin\theta$):

$$-r_2 \omega_2 \sin\theta_2 - r_3 \omega_3 \sin\theta_3 = -r_4 \omega_4 \sin\theta_4$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the Y-component similarly:**

$$r_2 \omega_2 \cos\theta_2 + r_3 \omega_3 \cos\theta_3 = r_4 \omega_4 \cos\theta_4$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into a matrix form** (collect $\omega_3$ and $\omega_4$ on the left):

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} -r_3\sin\theta_3 & \quad r_4\sin\theta_4 \\ \phantom{-}r_3\cos\theta_3 & -r_4\cos\theta_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \omega_3 \\ \omega_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} r_2\omega_2\sin\theta_2 \\ -r_2\omega_2\cos\theta_2 \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Solve by Cramer's rule** to get $\omega_3$ and $\omega_4$ for any given $\theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "How" behind the matrix:** This is the **analytical matrix** referred to in your syllabus. Each column corresponds to a moving joint, and each row corresponds to a Cartesian direction. The same template works for any planar linkage.

## 3.3 Derivation of Slider-Crank Position Equation

**Step 1 — Vector loop for a centered slider-crank:**

$$\vec{r} + \vec{l} - \vec{x} = 0$$

**Step 2 — X and Y components:**

$$r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi = x$$

$$r\sin\theta - l\sin\phi = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = \arcsin\left(\frac{r\sin\theta}{l}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\cos\phi = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\phi}$** into the X-equation:

$$x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{l^2 - r^2 \sin^2\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. time** to get velocity:

$$\dot{x} = -r\omega\sin\theta + \frac{-r^2\omega\sin\theta\cos\theta}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}$$

**Step 5 — Factor and simplify** (this is the standard form):

$$\dot{x} = -r\omega\sin\theta \left(1 + \frac{r\cos\theta}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Differentiate again for acceleration:**

$$\ddot{x} = -r\omega^2\cos\theta - r\alpha\sin\theta - \frac{r^2\omega^2\cos^2\theta + r^2\omega^2\sin^2\theta \left(\frac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{l^2}\right)}{(l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta)^{1/2}} \cdot \text{(full expansion)}$$

The standard engineering simplification is:

$$\ddot{x} = -r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r\cos 2\theta}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}} + \frac{r^3\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta}{(l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta)^{3/2}}\right) - r\alpha\sin\theta\left(1 + \frac{r\cos\theta}{\sqrt{l^2-r^2\sin^2\theta}}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that the **second term inside the bracket** arises from the changing direction of the connecting rod. This Coriolis-like effect is *not* zero even if $\alpha = 0$.

## 3.4 The Complete Analytical Matrix for a 4-Bar Mechanism

In a computer simulation loop, the position, velocity, and acceleration form a **3-tier analytical matrix stack**:

| Tier | Matrix Form | Linear? | Solver |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Position** | Non-linear trig equations in $\theta_3, \theta_4$ | **No** (transcendental) | Newton-Raphson / Freudenstein closed-form |
| **Velocity** | $\begin{bmatrix} -r_3\sin\theta_3 & r_4\sin\theta_4 \\ r_3\cos\theta_3 & -r_4\cos\theta_4 \end{bmatrix} \{\omega\} = \{D\}$ | **Yes** | Direct inverse (Cramer's rule) |
| **Acceleration** | $\begin{bmatrix} -r_3\sin\theta_3 & r_4\sin\theta_4 \\ r_3\cos\theta_3 & -r_4\cos\theta_4 \end{bmatrix} \{\alpha\} = \{E\}$ | **Yes** | Direct inverse |

**Where the RHS vectors are:**

$$\{D\} = \begin{Bmatrix} r_2\omega_2\sin\theta_2 \\ -r_2\omega_2\cos\theta_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

$$\{E\} = \begin{Bmatrix} r_2\alpha_2\sin\theta_2 + r_2\omega_2^2\cos\theta_2 + r_3\omega_3^2\cos\theta_3 - r_4\omega_4^2\cos\theta_4 \\ -r_2\alpha_2\cos\theta_2 + r_2\omega_2^2\sin\theta_2 + r_3\omega_3^2\sin\theta_3 - r_4\omega_4^2\sin\theta_4 \end{Bmatrix}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** The **coefficient matrix is the same** for both velocity and acceleration — only the RHS changes! This is why CAE software can re-use the matrix inverse from one tier to the next, saving computation time.

## 3.5 Algorithm for Computer-Aided Kinematic Simulation

```
Step 1: INPUT link lengths r1, r2, r3, r4, angular step Δθ2, ω2, α2
Step 2: SET θ2 = 0
Step 3: REPEAT until θ2 = 360°:
        a) SOLVE position eqns. → get θ3, θ4
        b) SOLVE velocity matrix → get ω3, ω4
        c) SOLVE acceleration matrix → get α3, α4
        d) STORE (θ2, θ3, θ4, ω3, ω4, α3, α4)
        e) UPDATE θ2 = θ2 + Δθ2
Step 4: OUTPUT displacement, velocity, acceleration tables
Step 5: PLOT graphs (Bode plots, displacement vs. θ2, etc.)
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Four-Bar Mechanism — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **kinematic data flow** of a four-bar simulation. Each block represents a computational step in the simulation loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: User Inputs r1 r2 r3 r4 omega2] --> B[Set initial theta2 = 0 deg]
    B --> C[Increment theta2 by delta_theta]
    C --> D{Position Analysis}
    D -->|Solve trig eqns| E[theta3 and theta4]
    E --> F{Velocity Analysis}
    F -->|Solve 2x2 matrix| G[omega3 and omega4]
    G --> H{Acceleration Analysis}
    H -->|Solve 2x2 matrix| I[alpha3 and alpha4]
    I --> J[Store all values in array]
    J --> K{theta2 less than 360}
    K -->|YES| C
    K -->|NO| L[Output: Tables and Graphs]
    L --> M[Plot theta3 vs theta2]
    L --> N[Plot omega4 vs theta2]
    L --> O[Plot alpha3 vs theta2]
    M --> P[END Simulation]
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Slider-Crank Mechanism — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[START: User Inputs r l omega offset] --> T[Set theta = 0 deg]
    T --> U[Increment theta by delta_theta]
    U --> V{Compute phi from sin inverse}
    V --> W[Compute x = r cos theta + l cos phi]
    W --> X[Compute v = dx dt]
    X --> Y[Compute a = dv dt]
    Y --> Z[Store in table]
    Z --> AA{theta less than 720}
    AA -->|YES| U
    AA -->|NO| AB[Plot piston position vs theta]
    AB --> AC[Plot piston velocity vs theta]
    AB --> AD[Plot piston acceleration vs theta]
    AC --> AE[END Simulation]
    AD --> AE
```

## 4.3 Physical Schematic — Four-Bar Mechanism (Mermaid Block Representation)

Since Mermaid cannot render the physical geometry precisely, the following block diagram conveys the **linkage topology and joint types** of a four-bar mechanism. Each block is a link; each edge is a revolute joint.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Ground Link r1] ---|Revolute Joint| A[Crank r2]
    A ---|Revolute Joint| B[Coupler r3]
    B ---|Revolute Joint| C[Rocker r4]
    C ---|Revolute Joint| G
```

> [!NOTE]
> In this topological map, the four blocks $G, A, B, C$ correspond to the four physical links, and the four edges represent the **four pin joints**. The cycle $G \to A \to B \to C \to G$ is the closed kinematic chain.

## 4.4 Physical Schematic — Slider-Crank Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Ground Pivot] ---|Revolute| A1[Crank r]
    A1 ---|Revolute| B1[Connecting Rod l]
    B1 ---|Revolute| C1[Slider Piston]
    C1 -->|Prismatic| G2[Slider Guide]
    G2 ---|Fixed| G1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference:** The slider-crank has **3 revolute joints + 1 prismatic joint**, whereas the four-bar has **4 revolute joints**. The prismatic joint introduces **one translational DOF** instead of a rotational one.

## 4.5 Coupler Point Trajectory — Why We Simulate

A key deliverable of kinematic simulation is the **tracing of coupler points** (e.g., point P on link $r_3$). This produces complex **coupler curves** useful in:

- **Six-bar straight-line mechanisms** (Watt, Peaucellier)
- **Quick-return mechanisms** (Whitworth, crank-shaper)
- **Path generators** (walking machines, robot end-effectors)

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define kinematic simulation of a mechanism. List the three tiers of kinematic analysis in the order they are computed.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Kinematic simulation** is the computer-aided process of determining the motion (positions, velocities, and accelerations) of all links in a mechanism as the input link moves through its cycle, **without considering the forces** that produce the motion.
>
> The three tiers of kinematic analysis are:
>
> 1. **Position Analysis** — Determines angular/linear displacements of all links (involves solving non-linear trigonometric equations like the loop-closure equation or Freudenstein's equation).
>
> 2. **Velocity Analysis** — First time-derivative of position; yields angular and linear velocities (solved as a **linear** matrix equation after positions are known).
>
> 3. **Acceleration Analysis** — Second time-derivative of position; yields angular and linear accelerations (again a **linear** matrix equation, using the same coefficient matrix as velocity but a different RHS).
>
> *Board-Exam Tip:* Always state the order **P → V → A** explicitly. Each step *requires* the previous step's results.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Freudenstein's equation for a four-bar mechanism. Define the three constants $K_1, K_2, K_3$.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Freudenstein's Equation** gives a direct closed-form trigonometric solution for the output angle $\theta_3$ of a four-bar mechanism in terms of the input angle $\theta_2$:
>
> $$K_1\cos\theta_3 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3)$$
>
> The three constants are dimensionless ratios of the link lengths:
>
> $$K_1 = \frac{r_4}{r_2}, \quad K_2 = \frac{r_1}{r_2}, \quad K_3 = \frac{r_1^2 - r_2^2 + r_3^2 + r_4^2}{2 r_2 r_4}$$
>
> *Board-Exam Tip:* Mention that this is the **classical direct position solution** (no iteration needed) and is widely used in **synthesis** (designing mechanisms for prescribed motion).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice]`

**For a four-bar mechanism with link lengths $r_1 = 100$ mm (ground), $r_2 = 30$ mm (crank), $r_3 = 90$ mm (coupler), and $r_4 = 80$ mm (rocker):**

#### (a) **(7 Marks)** Verify Grashof's Law and classify the mechanism. Find the position of the output link $\theta_4$ when the input crank is at $\theta_2 = 60°$. Take $\theta_1 = 0°$.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify shortest (S) and longest (L) links:**
- $r_1 = 100$ → Longest = $L = 100$ mm
- $r_2 = 30$ → Shortest = $S = 30$ mm
- $r_3 = 90$ mm, $r_4 = 80$ mm (P and Q)

**Step 2 — Apply Grashof's Law:**

$$S + L = 30 + 100 = 130 \text{ mm}$$
$$P + Q = 90 + 80 = 170 \text{ mm}$$

Since $S + L < P + Q$ (130 < 170), the inequality is satisfied strictly.

$$\therefore \text{Class: } \mathbf{Grashof \, Class \, I \, Crank-Rocker}$$

The shortest link ($r_2$) is the **crank** and it can fully rotate.

**Step 3 — Compute the angle difference $\theta_2 - \theta_1$:**

$$\theta_2 - \theta_1 = 60° - 0° = 60°$$

**Step 4 — Apply the analytical position equation derived in Section 3.1:**

$$\cos(\theta_3 - \theta_4) = \frac{r_1^2 + r_2^2 - r_3^2 - r_4^2 + 2 r_1 r_2 \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_1)}{2 r_3 r_4}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 100^2 + 30^2 - 90^2 - 80^2 + 2(100)(30)\cos 60° \\
&= 10000 + 900 - 8100 - 6400 + 6000(0.5) \\
&= 10900 - 14500 + 3000 \\
&= -600
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute denominator:**

$$\text{Denominator} = 2(90)(80) = 14400$$

**Step 7 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\cos(\theta_3 - \theta_4) = \frac{-600}{14400} = -0.04167$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $\theta_3 - \theta_4$:**

$$\theta_3 - \theta_4 = \arccos(-0.04167) = 92.39°$$

This is the *angle of the coupler relative to the rocker* at this crank position.

> **Note:** To get individual values of $\theta_3$ and $\theta_4$ (not just their difference), one additional piece of information is needed — typically an "assembly mode" assumption or a sign convention. For a full mark, complete both unknowns by setting the assembly mode and back-substituting into the original X-Y equations.

---

#### (b) **(7 Marks)** Derive the velocity and acceleration coefficient matrices for this four-bar mechanism in symbolic form. Explain why the same coefficient matrix appears in both.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Position equations** (from Section 3.1):

$$r_2\cos\theta_2 + r_3\cos\theta_3 = r_4\cos\theta_4 + r_1\cos\theta_1$$

$$r_2\sin\theta_2 + r_3\sin\theta_3 = r_4\sin\theta_4 + r_1\sin\theta_1$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate once w.r.t. time** to get velocity:

$$-r_2\omega_2\sin\theta_2 - r_3\omega_3\sin\theta_3 + r_4\omega_4\sin\theta_4 = 0$$

$$r_2\omega_2\cos\theta_2 + r_3\omega_3\cos\theta_3 - r_4\omega_4\cos\theta_4 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Coefficient matrix for velocity:**

$$[C_v] = \begin{bmatrix} -r_3\sin\theta_3 & \quad r_4\sin\theta_4 \\ \phantom{-}r_3\cos\theta_3 & -r_4\cos\theta_4 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\{D\} = \begin{Bmatrix} r_2\omega_2\sin\theta_2 \\ -r_2\omega_2\cos\theta_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate again to get acceleration** (after using product rule on every term):

$$[C_a] = \begin{bmatrix} -r_3\sin\theta_3 & \quad r_4\sin\theta_4 \\ \phantom{-}r_3\cos\theta_3 & -r_4\cos\theta_4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Note that $[C_a] = [C_v]$! Only the RHS vector changes.

$$\{E\} = \begin{Bmatrix} r_2\alpha_2\sin\theta_2 + r_2\omega_2^2\cos\theta_2 + r_3\omega_3^2\cos\theta_3 - r_4\omega_4^2\cos\theta_4 \\ -r_2\alpha_2\cos\theta_2 + r_2\omega_2^2\sin\theta_2 + r_3\omega_3^2\sin\theta_3 - r_4\omega_4^2\sin\theta_4 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Why the matrices are the same:**
Both velocity and acceleration equations are obtained by **differentiating the same two position equations**. The first-order derivative of $\cos\theta$ gives $-\omega\sin\theta$ (a *linear* operator in $\omega$), and the second derivative gives $-\alpha\sin\theta - \omega^2\cos\theta$ (still *linear* in $\alpha$). Since differentiation is a linear operator, the **spatial derivative structure** (i.e., the coefficient of the unknown variable) is identical — only the *known* forcing terms (RHS) change.

This is the **reason CAE software can factorize $[C]$ once** and reuse it for both velocity and acceleration solutions, drastically reducing computation time.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2 Internal Choice]`

**For a slider-crank mechanism with crank radius $r = 50$ mm, connecting rod length $l = 200$ mm, and crank speed $\omega = 1500$ rpm (constant):**

#### (a) **(7 Marks)** Derive the expression for the slider position $x$ and slider velocity $v$ as functions of crank angle $\theta$. Compute the slider velocity at $\theta = 90°$ (TDC and BDC context).

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Vector loop (no offset):**

$$x = r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi$$
$$r\sin\theta = l\sin\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin\phi = \frac{r}{l}\sin\theta$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\cos\phi$ using $\cos^2\phi = 1 - \sin^2\phi$:**

$$\cos\phi = \sqrt{1 - \frac{r^2}{l^2}\sin^2\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting into the X-equation:**

$$x(\theta) = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. time** ($\dot\theta = \omega$):

$$\begin{aligned}
v(\theta) = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{dx}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega \cdot \frac{dx}{d\theta}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\frac{dx}{d\theta}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dx}{d\theta} &= -r\sin\theta + \frac{1}{2}\left(l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta\right)^{-1/2} \cdot (-2r^2\sin\theta\cos\theta) \\
&= -r\sin\theta - \frac{r^2\sin\theta\cos\theta}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}} \\
&= -r\sin\theta \left(1 + \frac{r\cos\theta}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Final velocity equation:**

$$v(\theta) = -r\omega \sin\theta \left(1 + \frac{r\cos\theta}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $\theta = 90°$:**

At $\theta = 90°$: $\sin 90° = 1$, $\cos 90° = 0$.

$$v(90°) = -r\omega (1)\left(1 + \frac{0}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2}}\right) = -r\omega$$

**Step 8 — Convert $\omega$ to rad/s:**

$$\omega = 1500 \text{ rpm} = 1500 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 9 — Compute numerical value:**

$$v = -r\omega = -0.050 \times 157.08 = -7.854 \text{ m/s}$$

The negative sign indicates the slider is moving in the negative X-direction (towards the crank center).

> **Physical interpretation:** At $\theta = 90°$ (crank horizontal, perpendicular to cylinder axis), the connecting rod has maximum angular velocity, and the slider reaches its **mean (mid) velocity**, between TDC ($\theta = 0°$, $v = 0$) and BDC ($\theta = 180°$, $v = 0$).

---

#### (b) **(7 Marks)** Draw a labeled block diagram of the complete kinematic simulation loop for the slider-crank mechanism. Explain each block's function in the algorithm.

**Model Solution:**

A complete kinematic simulation algorithm consists of **5 main blocks**, executed cyclically:

| Block # | Block Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Input** | User provides link lengths $r, l$, crank speed $\omega$, time step $\Delta t$, offset $d$ (if any), and the number of cycles. |
| **2** | **Position Computation** | For each time step, solve the two non-linear position equations $r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi = x$ and $r\sin\theta = l\sin\phi$ to get $\phi$ and $x$. |
| **3** | **Velocity Computation** | Differentiate the position equations once and solve the resulting **2×2 linear system** for $\dot{\phi}$ and $\dot{x}$. |
| **4** | **Acceleration Computation** | Differentiate the velocity equations and solve the **same 2×2 matrix** (with updated RHS) for $\ddot{\phi}$ and $\ddot{x}$. |
| **5** | **Output & Visualization** | Tabulate all results, plot $x$ vs $\theta$, $v$ vs $\theta$, and $a$ vs $\theta$. Optionally animate the mechanism frame-by-frame. |

**Block diagram (schematic of the iteration loop):**

```
        ┌──────────────┐
        │   INPUT      │
        │ (r, l, ω, Δt)│
        └──────┬───────┘
               │
               ▼
        ┌──────────────┐
        │  INCREMENT   │◄──────┐
        │   θ = θ+Δt   │       │
        └──────┬───────┘       │
               │               │
               ▼               │
        ┌──────────────┐       │
        │  POSITION    │       │
        │ ANALYSIS     │       │
        └──────┬───────┘       │
               │               │
               ▼               │
        ┌──────────────┐       │
        │  VELOCITY    │       │
        │ ANALYSIS     │       │
        └──────┬───────┘       │
               │               │
               ▼               │
        ┌──────────────┐       │
        │ ACCELERATION │       │
        │ ANALYSIS     │       │
        └──────┬───────┘       │
               │               │
               ▼               │
        ┌──────────────┐       │
        │ θ < 720°?    │───YES─┘
        └──────┬───────┘
               │ NO
               ▼
        ┌──────────────┐
        │   OUTPUT     │
        │ (Tables,     │
        │  Graphs)     │
        └──────────────┘
```

> **Block-by-Block Logic (Why it works):**
> - The position step is **non-linear**, so it requires a special solver (e.g., Newton-Raphson or direct trigonometric inverse).
> - The velocity and acceleration steps are **linear** because differentiation of a trigonometric function w.r.t. time yields a term that is linear in the unknown $\omega$ or $\alpha$.
> - The same coefficient matrix is reused — a major efficiency gain in computer simulation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the assembly mode:** When solving the four-bar position, there are two possible solutions for $\theta_3$ and $\theta_4$ ("open" and "crossed"). Students must **state the assembly mode** explicitly. KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if this is omitted.
>
> 2. **Mixing degrees and radians:** The trigonometric functions in the loop equation use **radians**, but crank positions are often given in **degrees**. Always convert $\theta$ to radians before substituting into $\sin$ and $\cos$. Common mistake: using $\sin(60°)$ directly in a formula expecting radians.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the $\omega^2$ terms in acceleration:** When differentiating the velocity equation a second time, the $\dfrac{d}{dt}(\omega \sin\theta)$ term produces **two** terms: $\alpha \sin\theta$ AND $\omega^2 \cos\theta$. Students often forget the $\omega^2 \cos\theta$ "centripetal" term. This costs **1–2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Confusing slider-crank offset sign:** The offset $d$ (perpendicular distance from crank center to slider line) has a sign convention. KTU expects it in the loop equation as $+d$ or $-d$ depending on whether the slider is *above* or *below* the crank center. A sign error here propagates through velocity and acceleration.
>
> 5. **Not stating the input convention:** Always state which link is the **driver** (input) and which is the **follower** (output). The analysis depends on this choice.
>
> 6. **Forgetting units in the formula sheet:** Examiners check if your final answer has the correct units (m/s, rad/s², etc.). Numerical values without units lose ½ mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **🧠 Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- [x] **Kinematic simulation = motion analysis only**, *no forces*. (Dynamic simulation includes forces.)

- [x] **Loop-Closure Equation** is the foundation: walk around the mechanism and the sum of position vectors = zero.

- [x] **Four-Bar Vector Loop:** $\vec{r_1} - \vec{r_2} - \vec{r_3} + \vec{r_4} = 0$ (signs depend on chosen traversal direction).

- [x] **Slider-Crank Vector Loop:** $\vec{r} + \vec{l} - \vec{x} = 0$ (no offset case).

- [x] **Three Tiers in order:** Position (non-linear) → Velocity (linear) → Acceleration (linear).

- [x] **Coefficient matrix $[C]$ is the SAME for velocity and acceleration** — only RHS changes.

- [x] **Freudenstein's Equation** gives a direct closed-form solution for four-bar position:

$$K_1\cos\theta_3 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3)$$

- [x] **Grashof's Law** $S + L \le P + Q$ determines whether at least one link can fully rotate.

- [x] **Assembly Mode** must always be specified (open vs. crossed).

- [x] **Velocity matrix** is $2 \times 2$ for a four-bar; solved by Cramer's rule or matrix inversion.

- [x] **Slider velocity** = $\dot{x} = -r\omega\sin\theta\left(1 + \dfrac{r\cos\theta}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}\right)$.

- [x] **Slider acceleration** has BOTH $\omega^2$ (centripetal) and $\alpha$ (tangential) terms — don't forget either.

- [x] **Time step $\Delta\theta$** typically 1° to 5° for accurate simulation.

- [x] **CAe software** (MATLAB, Adams, Working Model) implements this loop automatically — but the *equations* in the software are exactly what was derived above.

- [x] **Real-world uses:** IC engines (slider-crank), suspensions (four-bar), sewing machines, walking robots, packaging machinery.

- [x] **Units mantra:** Lengths in **mm or m**, angles in **radians** (in the trig functions) or **degrees** (in the input specification), velocities in **m/s**, angular velocities in **rad/s**, accelerations in **m/s²** or **rad/s²**.

---

✅ **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: Kinematic Simulation of Four Bar and Slider Crank Mechanisms**

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Stress Analysis of Simple Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL407 - COMPUTER AIDED MODELING AND SIMULATION OF MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Static Stress Analysis of Simple Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Stress Analysis** is a structural analysis methodology that evaluates the response of a structure to time-independent (static) loading conditions, where inertial and damping effects are neglected. In the context of **CAE (Computer-Aided Engineering)**, static stress analysis utilizes the **Finite Element Method (FEM)** to numerically solve the governing equilibrium equation:
>
> $$\mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{F}$$
>
> where **K** is the global stiffness matrix, **u** is the nodal displacement vector, and **F** is the applied force vector. The objective is to determine displacements, strains, and stresses throughout the structure under prescribed loads and boundary conditions.

The term "**Simple Structures**" in the KTU laboratory framework typically refers to canonical mechanical components such as:
- **Cantilever beams** (with point or distributed loads)
- **Simply supported beams** (with central point loads)
- **Stepped shafts** under torsional or bending loads
- **L-brackets, plates with holes, and trusses**

These structures are chosen because they have **closed-form analytical solutions** (from **Strength of Materials / Mechanics of Materials**), enabling direct **validation of CAE results** against theoretical benchmarks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Sponge and the Truss"**
>
> Imagine a **trampoline** with a person standing at its center. The trampoline fabric represents the structure, the person represents the load, and the springs around the frame represent the boundary conditions (supports). Static stress analysis in CAE works similarly:
> - The software **divides the trampoline fabric** into thousands of tiny triangular patches (this is **meshing**).
> - Each patch is treated as a tiny spring system with **stiffness properties**.
> - The software then asks: *"Given the person's weight (load) and the fixed frame (boundary), how much does each patch stretch, and how much internal force does it carry?"*
> - The answer gives us **displacements** (how much the fabric sags) and **stresses** (how stretched each patch is).
>
> In real engineering, instead of a trampoline, we have a steel beam, and instead of a person, we have a force. The computer does the exact same math — just in 3D and with much more rigorous physics.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!NOTE]
> Key **Engineering Constants & Units** for Static Stress Analysis:
> - **Young's Modulus (E)**: For structural steel **E = 200 GPa = 200 × 10⁹ N/m² = 2 × 10⁵ MPa**
> - **Poisson's Ratio (ν)**: For structural steel **ν = 0.30**
> - **Density (ρ)**: For structural steel **ρ = 7850 kg/m³** (used only in self-weight / thermal)
> - **Acceleration due to gravity (g)**: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - **Yield Strength (σ_y) for Mild Steel**: **σ_y = 250 MPa**
> - **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_uts)**: **σ_uts = 410 MPa**

### 1.4 Why "Static" and Not "Dynamic"?

| Aspect | Static Analysis | Dynamic Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| **Time dependence** | Loads are constant in time | Loads vary with time |
| **Inertia forces** | Neglected (assumed zero) | Included (ma ≠ 0) |
| **Damping** | Not considered | Considered |
| **Equation solved** | **[K]{u} = {F}** | **[M]{ü} + [C]{u̇} + [K]{u} = {F(t)}** |
| **Typical application** | Bridges, building frames under gravity | Turbine blades, earthquake loads |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module Experiment 2, the analysis is restricted to **linear-elastic, isotropic, homogeneous materials** with **small deformation theory** (i.e., geometric nonlinearity is neglected). This is the most fundamental CAE experiment and forms the bedrock for all advanced simulations.

---

## 1.5 Pre-Analysis Checklist (CAE Workflow Map)

Before any CAE simulation, the engineer must answer the **Five Ws of FEA**:

1. **WHAT** am I analyzing? (Geometry, material, domain)
2. **WHY** does it need analysis? (Design verification, failure prediction, optimization)
3. **WHERE** are the loads and constraints? (Boundary conditions)
4. **WHICH** physics is relevant? (Linear static, thermal, modal…)
5. **HOW** accurate must the solution be? (Mesh density, element order, convergence)

These questions map directly to the **KTU 2024 Course Outcomes (COs)**:
- **CO1**: Apply CAE tools for engineering problem solving
- **CO2**: Interpret and validate simulation results
- **CO3**: Demonstrate competency in pre/post-processing

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Finite Element Method — Theoretical Foundation

The **Finite Element Method (FEM)** is a numerical technique for solving boundary value problems in engineering. For static stress analysis, the procedure follows a rigorous mathematical sequence:

### Step 1: Discretization (Meshing)
The continuous domain (the actual beam) is subdivided into a finite number of **smaller sub-domains** called **elements** (tetrahedra, hexahedra, or shells in 3D; triangles or quadrilaterals in 2D). The corners of these elements are called **nodes**.

> **Why discretize?** The exact analytical solution of a partial differential equation (PDE) governing elasticity is **infeasible for complex geometries**. By breaking the domain into small pieces, we can **approximate** the solution within each element using simple polynomial functions (called **shape functions**).

### Step 2: Selection of Displacement Function (Shape Function)
For each element, the displacement field **u(x)** is approximated as:

$$\mathbf{u}(x) \approx \mathbf{N}(x) \cdot \mathbf{d}_e$$

where:
- **N(x)** = Shape function matrix (interpolates displacements from nodes)
- **d_e** = Vector of nodal displacements for element *e*

For a **linear 1D bar element** (2-node), the shape function is:
$$N_1(x) = 1 - \frac{x}{L}, \quad N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L}$$

### Step 3: Derivation of Element Stiffness Matrix
The **strain-displacement relation** is:
$$\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{d}_e$$

where **B** is the **strain-displacement matrix** (derivative of shape functions).

The **stress-strain relation** (constitutive law, Hooke's Law) is:
$$\boldsymbol{\sigma} = \mathbf{D} \cdot \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \mathbf{D} \cdot \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{d}_e$$

where **D** is the **constitutive (material property) matrix**.

Applying the **Principle of Minimum Potential Energy**, the element stiffness matrix is:

$$\mathbf{k}_e = \int_{V_e} \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{D} \, \mathbf{B} \, dV$$

> **Physical meaning of k_e:** It is a matrix that maps nodal displacements to nodal forces for a single element. It encodes the geometry (B), the material stiffness (D), and the size (dV integration).

### Step 4: Assembly of Global Equations
Element matrices are assembled into the **global stiffness matrix K** using connectivity information from the mesh. The final equilibrium equation is:

$$\mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{F}$$

### Step 5: Application of Boundary Conditions
Two types of boundary conditions are imposed:
- **Essential (Dirichlet) BCs**: Prescribed displacements (e.g., *u = 0* at fixed support)
- **Natural (Neumann) BCs**: Prescribed forces/tractions (e.g., *F = 1000 N* at free end)

### Step 6: Solution for Nodal Displacements
The linear system **[K]{u} = {F}** is solved using direct solvers (e.g., **Gaussian elimination, Cholesky decomposition**) or iterative solvers (e.g., **Conjugate Gradient**).

### Step 7: Post-Processing (Strain and Stress Recovery)
Once nodal displacements are known, strains and stresses are computed at integration points (or extrapolated to nodes) using the strain-displacement and constitutive relations.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula repository** for Static Stress Analysis. These are the equations that KTU examiners expect students to write during theory and lab viva voce.

### Table 2.1: Master Equation Sheet for Static Linear FEA

| **S.No.** | **Formula / Equation** | **Description** | **Units (SI)** |
|:---------:|:----------------------|:----------------|:--------------:|
| 1 | $\mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{F}$ | Global equilibrium equation | [N/m]·[m] = [N] |
| 2 | $\mathbf{k}_e = \int_{V_e} \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{D} \, \mathbf{B} \, dV$ | Element stiffness matrix derivation | N/m |
| 3 | $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{d}_e$ | Strain-displacement relation | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\boldsymbol{\sigma} = \mathbf{D} \cdot \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ | Stress-strain (Hooke's law) | Pa (N/m²) |
| 5 | $\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]}$ | Von Mises equivalent stress | Pa |
| 6 | $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3 =$ roots of $\sigma^3 - I_1\sigma^2 + I_2\sigma - I_3 = 0$ | Principal stresses (eigenvalues) | Pa |
| 7 | $I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z$ | First stress invariant | Pa |
| 8 | $I_2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y\sigma_z + \sigma_z\sigma_x - \tau_{xy}^2 - \tau_{yz}^2 - \tau_{zx}^2$ | Second stress invariant | Pa² |
| 9 | $I_3 = \sigma_x\sigma_y\sigma_z + 2\tau_{xy}\tau_{yz}\tau_{zx} - \sigma_x\tau_{yz}^2 - \sigma_y\tau_{zx}^2 - \sigma_z\tau_{xy}^2$ | Third stress invariant | Pa³ |
| 10 | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{FL^3}{3EI}$ | Max deflection of cantilever beam (tip load F) | m |
| 11 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Max bending stress (flexure formula) | Pa |
| 12 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{c} = \dfrac{bh^2}{6}$ (rectangular) | Section modulus | m³ |
| 13 | $I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12}$ (rectangular) | Second moment of area | m⁴ |
| 14 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$ | Max shear stress in torsion (shaft) | Pa |
| 15 | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ (solid shaft) | Polar moment of inertia | m⁴ |
| 16 | $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ | Angle of twist | radians |
| 17 | $FOS = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{vm, max}}$ | Factor of Safety (against yielding) | dimensionless |
| 18 | $\sigma_{critical} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{(KL)^2}$ | Euler's buckling load (columns) | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM NOTE:** Always quote the **units** explicitly. KTU examiners award **1 mark** for dimensional consistency in derivations. A formula written without units in the final answer may cost 0.5 to 1 mark.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Static stress analysis is the **backbone of design verification** in virtually every industry:
> - **Aerospace**: Wing spar analysis under cruise loads (Boeing 787 uses ~1 million FEM elements)
> - **Automotive**: Chassis frame stiffness and crash-relevant static load cases
> - **Civil**: Bridge girders, building columns, offshore platforms
> - **Pressure Vessels**: ASME Section VIII code compliance using Von Mises stress
> - **Biomedical**: Orthopedic implant stress shielding analysis
> - **Electronics**: PCB warpage and BGA solder joint reliability

> The **Von Mises stress criterion** is the **most widely used yield criterion** for ductile materials (steel, aluminum, copper). It is derived from the **distortion energy theory** (Hencky–von Mises), which states that yielding begins when the **distortion strain energy** per unit volume equals that at the yield point in a uniaxial tension test.

---

## 2.4 The Three Stress Invariants — Why They Matter

The three **stress invariants** (I₁, I₂, I₃) are combinations of the six independent stress components (σx, σy, σz, τxy, τyz, τzx) that **do not change** with the orientation of the coordinate system. They are essential because:

1. **I₁ represents the hydrostatic (volumetric) stress component** — responsible for volume change, not yielding.
2. **I₂ and I₃ contain the deviatoric (shape-changing) component** — responsible for yielding and fracture.
3. The **principal stresses** (σ₁, σ₂, σ₃) are the **eigenvalues** of the stress tensor, and the **principal directions** are the corresponding eigenvectors.

> **Why use principal stresses?** A stress state at a point can be expressed in **infinitely many coordinate systems** (by rotating axes). The principal stress representation is **unique** and **coordinate-invariant**, making it ideal for failure criteria.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Stiffness Matrix of a 1D Bar Element

This is the **most fundamental derivation** in FEA and is **frequently asked in KTU viva voce and written exams**. We will derive it with **every algebraic step** explicit.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a **uniform bar element** of:
- Length: **L**
- Cross-sectional area: **A**
- Young's Modulus: **E**

The element has **two nodes** (Node 1 and Node 2), each with **one degree of freedom** (axial displacement along x).

**Nodal displacement vector:**
$$\mathbf{d}_e = \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Nodal force vector:**
$$\mathbf{f}_e = \begin{Bmatrix} f_1 \\ f_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Define Shape Functions

The displacement field within the element is assumed to be **linear**:

$$u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x$$

At **x = 0** (Node 1): $u(0) = u_1 \Rightarrow a_0 = u_1$

At **x = L** (Node 2): $u(L) = u_2 \Rightarrow a_1 = \dfrac{u_2 - u_1}{L}$

Substituting back:

$$u(x) = u_1 + \dfrac{u_2 - u_1}{L} \cdot x = \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{L}\right) u_1 + \left(\dfrac{x}{L}\right) u_2$$

Therefore, the **shape functions** are:

$$N_1(x) = 1 - \dfrac{x}{L}, \qquad N_2(x) = \dfrac{x}{L}$$

In matrix form:

$$u(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - \dfrac{x}{L} & \dfrac{x}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \mathbf{N}(x) \cdot \mathbf{d}_e$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Strain-Displacement Matrix (B)

The **axial strain** is:

$$\varepsilon(x) = \dfrac{du}{dx} = \dfrac{d}{dx}\left[\left(1 - \dfrac{x}{L}\right) u_1 + \left(\dfrac{x}{L}\right) u_2\right]$$

Differentiating term by term:

$$\varepsilon(x) = -\dfrac{1}{L} u_1 + \dfrac{1}{L} u_2$$

In matrix form:

$$\varepsilon(x) = \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{1}{L} & \dfrac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{d}_e$$

So the **strain-displacement matrix** is:

$$\mathbf{B} = \dfrac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Constitutive Matrix (D)

For 1D linear elasticity, Hooke's law gives:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbf{D} = E \quad (\text{a scalar in 1D})$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Element Stiffness Matrix

Using the formula:

$$\mathbf{k}_e = \int_{V_e} \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{D} \, \mathbf{B} \, dV = \int_0^L \mathbf{B}^T E \mathbf{B} \, A \, dx$$

Compute $\mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{B}$:

$$\mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{B} = \dfrac{1}{L}\begin{Bmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} \cdot \dfrac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{L^2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiply by **D = E** and **A**:

$$\mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{D} \, \mathbf{B} = \dfrac{EA}{L^2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Integrate from **0 to L**:

$$\mathbf{k}_e = \dfrac{EA}{L^2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \int_0^L dx = \dfrac{EA}{L^2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot L$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{k}_e = \dfrac{EA}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **famous bar element stiffness matrix** — it appears in almost every FEA textbook and KTU exam. Memorize its structure: **diagonal terms are positive, off-diagonal are negative, magnitude is EA/L**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: Cantilever Beam — Analytical vs. FEA

This is the **most likely KTU lab experiment scenario**. We will solve the same problem by **two methods** (Strength of Materials and FEA) and compare.

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> A **cantilever beam** of length **L = 1 m**, rectangular cross-section **b = 25 mm** (width) × **h = 10 mm** (depth), made of **structural steel (E = 200 GPa, ν = 0.3)**, is fixed at one end (x = 0) and subjected to a **point load F = 100 N** at the free end (x = L), directed downward (−y). Determine:
> (a) Maximum deflection
> (b) Maximum bending stress
> (c) Compare with ANSYS/SolidWorks Simulation FEA result

### 3.2.2 Given Data

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Length | L | 1 m = 1000 mm |
| Width | b | 25 mm = 0.025 m |
| Depth (height) | h | 10 mm = 0.010 m |
| Young's Modulus | E | 200 × 10⁹ Pa = 200 GPa |
| Poisson's Ratio | ν | 0.30 |
| Point Load at tip | F | 100 N (downward) |

### 3.2.3 Part (a): Maximum Deflection — Analytical (Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory)

The **maximum deflection** of a cantilever beam with an end point load occurs at the **free end (x = L)** and is given by:

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{F L^3}{3 E I}$$

We must first compute the **second moment of area** I for the rectangular cross-section about the **neutral axis (horizontal centroidal axis)**:

$$I = \dfrac{b h^3}{12}$$

Substituting the values:

$$I = \dfrac{(0.025)(0.010)^3}{12} = \dfrac{(0.025)(1 \times 10^{-6})}{12} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-8}}{12}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$2.5 \times 10^{-8} \div 12 = 0.20833... \times 10^{-8} = 2.0833 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^4$$

Therefore:

$$I = 2.0833 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^4$$

Now compute the deflection:

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{(100)(1)^3}{3 \times (200 \times 10^9) \times (2.0833 \times 10^{-9})}$$

**Step-by-step evaluation of the denominator:**

$$3 \times 200 \times 10^9 = 600 \times 10^9 = 6 \times 10^{11}$$

$$6 \times 10^{11} \times 2.0833 \times 10^{-9} = 6 \times 2.0833 \times 10^{11-9} = 12.5 \times 10^{2} = 1250$$

Now the deflection:

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{100}{1250} = 0.08 \, \text{m} = 80 \, \text{mm}$$

> **Analytical result:** $\delta_{max} = 0.08 \, \text{m} = 80 \, \text{mm}$

### 3.2.4 Part (b): Maximum Bending Stress — Analytical

The **maximum bending moment** in a cantilever with end point load occurs at the **fixed end**:

$$M_{max} = F \cdot L = 100 \times 1 = 100 \, \text{N·m}$$

The **maximum bending stress** (at the top or bottom fiber, where y = c = h/2) is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z}$$

where the **section modulus** Z is:

$$Z = \dfrac{I}{c} = \dfrac{I}{h/2} = \dfrac{2I}{h}$$

For a rectangular cross-section, a closed-form expression is:

$$Z = \dfrac{b h^2}{6}$$

Substituting values:

$$Z = \dfrac{(0.025)(0.010)^2}{6} = \dfrac{(0.025)(1 \times 10^{-4})}{6} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-6}}{6} = 4.1667 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^3$$

Now the bending stress:

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z} = \dfrac{100}{4.1667 \times 10^{-7}}$$

Evaluating:

$$\dfrac{100}{4.1667 \times 10^{-7}} = 100 \times 2.4 \times 10^{6} = 2.4 \times 10^{8} \, \text{Pa} = 240 \, \text{MPa}$$

> **Analytical result:** $\sigma_{max} = 240 \, \text{MPa}$

### 3.2.5 Part (c): Validation Against FEA (ANSYS Workbench / SolidWorks Simulation)

**Step 1 — Geometry**: Create a 3D beam in ANSYS DesignModeler or SolidWorks Part mode. Dimensions as given.

**Step 2 — Material**: Structural Steel (E = 200 GPa, ν = 0.3).

**Step 3 — Meshing**: Use a **tetrahedral mesh** with element size = 5 mm (≈ 200 elements per beam length). For convergence, refine to 2 mm and compare.

**Step 4 — Boundary Conditions**:
- **Fixed Support** at x = 0 face (all 6 DOFs constrained: u = v = w = 0)
- **Force** of 100 N in −y direction at the free-end face

**Step 5 — Solve** and read the following probes:
- **Total Deformation** at the free end (max)
- **Equivalent (Von Mises) Stress** at the fixed end (max)

**Expected FEA Results (with fine mesh):**

| Quantity | Analytical | FEA (Fine Mesh) | % Error |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max Deflection | 80.00 mm | ≈ 79.92 mm | < 0.1% |
| Max Von Mises Stress | 240.00 MPa | ≈ 239.5 MPa | < 0.3% |
| Max Principal Stress (σ₁) | 240.00 MPa (tension) | ≈ 240.2 MPa | < 0.2% |
| Min Principal Stress (σ₃) | 0 MPa (at free surface) | ≈ −0.5 MPa | — |

> **Validation statement:** The FEA results agree with analytical results to within 0.5%, which is excellent agreement for linear static analysis. Any deviation beyond 2–3% indicates a **mesh quality issue** or **incorrect boundary condition application**.

---

## 3.3 Convergence Study — A Critical CAE Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **mesh convergence study** is the process of progressively refining the mesh and observing how the solution changes. The **converged value** is the asymptotic limit of the FEA solution as the element size → 0.

For the cantilever beam example, a typical convergence table looks like:

| Element Size (mm) | Number of Elements | Max Deflection (mm) | Max Von Mises Stress (MPa) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 20 | 250 | 78.2 | 232.1 |
| 10 | 1,500 | 79.6 | 238.4 |
| 5 | 8,000 | 79.92 | 239.5 |
| 2 | 60,000 | 79.998 | 239.95 |
| 1 | 250,000 | 80.000 | 239.99 |
| **Analytical** | **∞** | **80.000** | **240.00** |

> **Engineering judgment:** A mesh size of 2–5 mm is typically **adequate for engineering-grade accuracy** (error < 0.5%) without excessive computational cost. Going finer than this yields diminishing returns.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Euler-Bernoulli Beam Equation (For Reference)

The governing differential equation for beam bending is:

$$EI \dfrac{d^4 y}{dx^4} = q(x)$$

where **q(x)** is the distributed load. For a **point load P at x = a** on a beam, the deflection is computed by integrating this equation four times, applying **four boundary conditions** (e.g., y = 0 and dy/dx = 0 at fixed end; M = 0 and V = 0 at free end for cantilever).

For our **cantilever with end load**, the result is:

$$y(x) = \dfrac{F x^2}{6 EI}(3L - x)$$

Setting **x = L**:

$$y(L) = \dfrac{F L^2}{6 EI}(3L - L) = \dfrac{F L^2}{6 EI} \cdot 2L = \dfrac{F L^3}{3 EI}$$

This confirms the deflection formula used in the worked example.

A **3D stress element** at a point has **6 independent stress components**: 3 normal (σx, σy, σz) and 3 shear (τxy, τyz, τzx). This is the foundation for understanding principal stress and Von Mises calculations.
