# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Path Planning and Trajectory Planning in the context of robotic manipulators. Illustrate with a one-line example.**

**Model Answer:**
Path planning is a **geometric** problem that determines the *shape* of a collision-free route from start to goal configuration, independent of time. Trajectory planning, in contrast, **parameterizes** the path with time, prescribing position, velocity, and acceleration profiles at every instant.

*Example:* Planning a smooth curve from point A to B on a CNC table is path planning; specifying the feed-rate so the tool traverses it in 4 seconds with zero start/end velocity is trajectory planning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key distinction word for exam:** *Geometry vs. Time-Parametrized Geometry.*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**State the role of the Jacobian matrix in motion simulation. What happens at a singular configuration?**

**Model Answer:**
The Jacobian $J(q)$ is the **first-order linear map** between joint-space velocities $\dot{q}$ and end-effector (task-space) velocities $\dot{x}$:

$$\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q}$$

It is essential in motion simulation for:
- Velocity propagation and force-torque transformation
- Singularity detection via $\det(J)=0$
- Inverse-rate kinematics $\dot{q} = J^{-1}\dot{x}$ when $J$ is invertible

At a **singular configuration**, $\det(J) = 0$, the manipulator loses one or more degrees of freedom in task space. Joint velocities must theoretically become **infinite** to achieve a finite Cartesian velocity, and the mechanism exhibits **loss of controllability**. Practically, the controller saturates and path deviation occurs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part B question is a **Module-Level Internal Choice**. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B, both of which carry **14 marks** divided as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Joint-Space Trajectory Generation*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** A single joint of a 6-DOF manipulator must move from $q_i = 20°$ to $q_f = 80°$ in $T = 5$ s with zero initial and final velocities. **(i)** Derive the cubic polynomial trajectory. **(ii)** Compute the joint position, velocity, and acceleration at $t = 1, 2.5, 4$ s. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**(i) Cubic Polynomial Derivation:**

General form: $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$

Boundary conditions:
- $q(0) = 20° \Rightarrow a_0 = 20$
- $\dot{q}(0) = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 = 0$
- $q(5) = 80° \Rightarrow 20 + 25 a_2 + 125 a_3 = 80 \Rightarrow 25 a_2 + 125 a_3 = 60$
- $\dot{q}(5) = 0 \Rightarrow 10 a_2 + 75 a_3 = 0 \Rightarrow a_2 = -7.5 a_3$

Substitute: $25(-7.5 a_3) + 125 a_3 = 60 \Rightarrow -187.5 a_3 + 125 a_3 = 60 \Rightarrow -62.5 a_3 = 60 \Rightarrow a_3 = -0.96$

Therefore: $a_2 = -7.5 \times (-0.96) = 7.2$

$$\boxed{q(t) = 20 + 7.2 t^2 - 0.96 t^3 \text{ (degrees)}}$$

**(ii) Evaluation Table:**

| $t$ (s) | $q(t)$ (°) | $\dot{q}(t)=14.4t-2.88t^2$ (°/s) | $\ddot{q}(t)=14.4-5.76t$ (°/s²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 26.24 | 11.52 | 8.64 |
| 2.5 | 50.00 | 18.00 | 0.00 |
| 4.0 | 73.76 | 11.52 | -8.64 |

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of cubic polynomial trajectory. Under what condition do you upgrade to a **quintic polynomial**? Show the standard form of the quintic trajectory and the additional boundary conditions it satisfies. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Advantages of cubic polynomial:**
1. Satisfies four boundary conditions (start/end position + velocity)
2. Yields continuous velocity profile (C¹ continuity)
3. Closed-form analytical solution, computationally cheap
4. Smooth enough for many low-speed industrial applications

**Limitations:**
1. **Acceleration is discontinuous at endpoints** ($t=0$ and $t=T$), exciting vibrations
2. Cannot prescribe acceleration boundary conditions
3. Not suitable for high-speed/high-precision applications (CNC machining, semiconductor handling)
4. Inflexible for multi-segment or blended trajectories

**Condition for upgrade:** When the application requires **C² continuity** (continuous acceleration) at the path endpoints — typical in CNC machining of molds, laser cutting, and **direct-drive robotic manipulators** where jerk-induced excitation must be eliminated.

**Standard quintic form:**

$$q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$$

**Six boundary conditions:**
1. $q(0) = q_i$
2. $q(T) = q_f$
3. $\dot{q}(0) = v_i$ (often 0)
4. $\dot{q}(T) = v_f$ (often 0)
5. $\ddot{q}(0) = a_i$ (often 0)
6. $\ddot{q}(T) = a_f$ (often 0)

This gives **6 equations for 6 unknowns**, yielding continuous position, velocity, and acceleration — hence bounded jerk.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Cartesian Path & 2R Inverse Kinematics*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** For a 2R planar manipulator with link lengths $l_1 = 0.5$ m, $l_2 = 0.4$ m: **(i)** Derive the inverse kinematics solution for the elbow-up configuration. **(ii)** Compute $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ for the end-effector position $(x, y) = (0.7, 0.4)$ m. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**(i) IK Derivation:**

The end-effector position equations (forward kinematics):

$$x = l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$
$$y = l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

Compute $x^2 + y^2$:

$$x^2 + y^2 = l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos\theta_2$$

Thus:

$$\cos\theta_2 = \frac{x^2 + y^2 - l_1^2 - l_2^2}{2 l_1 l_2} = \frac{0.49 + 0.16 - 0.25 - 0.16}{2(0.5)(0.4)} = \frac{0.24}{0.40} = 0.60$$

For **elbow-up**:
$$\theta_2 = +\arccos(0.60) = 53.13°$$

For $\theta_1$, use:

$$\theta_1 = \arctan2(y, x) - \arctan2(l_2 \sin\theta_2, l_1 + l_2 \cos\theta_2)$$

**(ii) Numerical Computation:**

$$\theta_2 = +53.13°$$
$$l_2 \sin\theta_2 = 0.4 \times \sin(53.13°) = 0.4 \times 0.8 = 0.32$$
$$l_1 + l_2 \cos\theta_2 = 0.5 + 0.4 \times 0.6 = 0.5 + 0.24 = 0.74$$
$$\arctan2(0.4, 0.7) = 29.74°$$
$$\arctan2(0.32, 0.74) = 23.41°$$

$$\theta_1 = 29.74° - 23.41° = 6.33°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_1 = 6.33°, \quad \theta_2 = 53.13°}$$

**(b)** Explain the **configuration space (C-space) approach** to path planning. With a suitable diagram description, discuss the **RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree)** algorithm and its **probabilistic completeness** property. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**C-space approach:**
The configuration of an n-DOF manipulator is uniquely described by a point in an n-dimensional **Configuration Space** ($C$-space). The robot's physical body sweeps the workspace as the configuration varies, and obstacles in the workspace are mapped into **C-space obstacles** ($C_{obs}$). The set of valid configurations is $C_{free} = C_{space} \setminus C_{obs}$. A path is a continuous curve $\gamma: [0,1] \to C_{free}$.

**RRT Algorithm:**
1. Initialize tree $\mathcal{T}$ with start configuration $q_{start}$
2. Sample a random configuration $q_{rand} \in C_{free}$
3. Find nearest node $q_{near} \in \mathcal{T}$ minimizing $\vert\vert q_{rand} - q_{near} \vert\vert$
4. Steer from $q_{near}$ toward $q_{rand}$ by step size $\Delta q$ to obtain $q_{new}$
5. If the local path $q_{near} \to q_{new}$ is collision-free, add $q_{new}$ to $\mathcal{T}$
6. Repeat until $q_{goal}$ is reached or within tolerance
7. Trace parent pointers from $q_{goal}$ to $q_{start}$ to extract path

**Probabilistic Completeness:**
RRT is **probabilistically complete**, meaning: *if a solution path exists, the probability that RRT finds it approaches 1 as the number of samples $N \to \infty$.* Formally:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} P(\text{RRT finds a solution} \vert \text{one exists}) = 1$$

**Limitations:**
- Not asymptotically optimal (use RRT* for that)
- Path may be jerky; post-processing smoothing (e.g., shortcutting) is often needed
- Narrow passages in C-space are hard to sample

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PCCPL407 Path Planning Problems:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Always convert degrees to radians (or vice versa) consistently. The coefficient $a_3$ has units of $°/s^3$ if $q$ is in degrees.
> 2. **Sign error in $\theta_2$:** Forgetting the elbow-up sign convention flips the entire solution.
> 3. **Wrong boundary conditions:** Some students apply velocity = max instead of = 0 at endpoints — read the question carefully.
> 4. **Cubic vs Quintic:** Knowing *when* to upgrade to a quintic is worth 2-3 marks explicitly.
> 5. **Trapezoidal profile:** Many students forget to check the *triangular threshold*; if the path is too short, the cruise phase does not exist.
> 6. **Jacobian singularity:** The condition is $\det(J) = 0$, not $J = 0$ — this is a frequently-failed trap.
> 7. **Mermaid / diagrams:** Always label axes and units; marks are reserved for clarity in KTU lab records and viva.
> 8. **No mention of workspace vs. C-space:** Examiners explicitly test this conceptual distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Last-Minute KTU Preparation**

- [ ] **Path Planning** = geometry only; **Trajectory Planning** = geometry + time
- [ ] **C-space** = abstract joint space; **Workspace** = physical Cartesian space
- [ ] **Forward Kinematics (FK):** Joint angles → End-effector pose
- [ ] **Inverse Kinematics (IK):** End-effector pose → Joint angles (multiple solutions, branches)
- [ ] **Jacobian $J$:** Maps joint velocity to task-space velocity; $\det(J) = 0$ ⇒ singularity
- [ ] **Cubic polynomial:** 4 boundary conditions (2 position, 2 velocity) → $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$
- [ ] **Quintic polynomial:** 6 boundary conditions (add 2 acceleration) → C² continuity, jerk bounded
- [ ] **Trapezoidal profile:** Accel → Cruise → Decel; check $d_{triangular} = v_{max}^2 / a_{max}$
- [ ] **S-curve profile:** Adds jerk-limited segments; smoothest but most complex
- [ ] **RRT:** Sampling-based, probabilistically complete, NOT optimal
- [ ] **RRT\*:** Asymptotically optimal variant with rewiring
- [ ] **A\* Search:** Heuristic-based graph search; admissible heuristic → optimal
- [ ] **Potential Field:** Gradient descent with attractive + repulsive forces; local minima possible
- [ ] **MBD equation:** $M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G = \tau$ — must be satisfied during dynamic motion simulation
- [ ] **Numerical integrators:** Euler, RK4, Adams-Bashforth; stability depends on timestep $dt$
- [ ] **Performance metrics:** Path accuracy, repeatability, cycle time, jerk limit
- [ ] **MATLAB functions to remember:** `tpoly()` (cubic), `lspb()` (trapezoidal), `mtraj()` (multi-axis), `jtraj()` (joint trajectory)
- [ ] **Software tools:** MATLAB/Simulink, ADAMS, ANSYS, RecurDyn, Visual Components
- [ ] **Standards:** ISO 9283 (robot performance), ISO 13849 (safety), OSHA 1910.212 (machine guarding)

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Path Planning and Motion Simulation**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, B.Tech Mechanical / Mechatronics Engineering*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing Push Button and Digital Sensor with Arduino or Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL407 - COMPUTER AIDED MODELING AND SIMULATION OF MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Interfacing Push Button and Digital Sensor with Arduino or Raspberry Pi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PCCPL407 – Computer Aided Modeling and Simulation of Mechanical Systems**, a **Digital Input Interface** refers to the hardware and software arrangement by which a binary-state physical signal (a *HIGH* / logic **1** or a *LOW* / logic **0** voltage level) from an electromechanical transducer is sampled, conditioned, and read by an embedded controller such as an **Arduino** (an AVR/ARM-based microcontroller development board) or a **Raspberry Pi** (a Broadcom BCM2837/2711 single-board computer with general-purpose input/output GPIO pins).

The two most common digital transducers in mechanical laboratory experiments are:

1. **Push Button Switch** – A *momentary mechanical contact device* that produces a clean DC voltage transition when actuated, and a spring-return to its idle state on release.
2. **Digital Sensor** – A solid-state transducer (e.g., PIR motion sensor, Hall-effect sensor, IR obstacle sensor, DS18B20 temperature sensor) whose output pin is a **TTL/CMOS-compatible logic level** rather than a continuously varying analog signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A *digital sensor* differs from an *analog sensor* in that its output is a discrete two-state logic signal. The interfacing task therefore reduces to **voltage-level matching**, **noise filtering (debounce)**, and **firmware polling / interrupt handling**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Letterbox and the Mailroom"

Imagine the Arduino or Raspberry Pi as a **post office mailroom clerk**. The push button is a **letterbox slot** on the office wall. When a person (the *user*) drops a letter (a *mechanical press*) into the slot:

- The letter physically *exists* or *does not exist* inside the box — there are only two states.
- The clerk does not continuously stare at the slot; instead, he **checks** it periodically (**polling mode**) or attaches a **bell** that rings when something is inserted (**interrupt mode**).
- If the letterbox flap bounces a few times before settling (this is exactly the *switch bounce* problem), the clerk might count "one letter, two letters, three letters…" incorrectly. To avoid this, the clerk waits a moment and checks again — this is **software debouncing**.

A digital sensor (PIR, IR, Hall sensor) acts like an **automated letter-delivery tube**: it announces "I detected motion" or "I did not detect motion" by toggling a logical wire to **5 V (HIGH)** or **0 V (LOW)**.

### 1.3 Key Operating Parameters of the Two Platforms

| Parameter | Arduino Uno (ATmega328P) | Raspberry Pi 4 (BCM2711) |
|---|---|---|
| Logic HIGH voltage | **5 V** | **3.3 V** |
| Logic LOW voltage | **0 V** | **0 V** |
| 5 V tolerance on GPIO | **Yes (5 V safe)** | **No — damages the SoC** |
| Internal pull-up resistor | **20 kΩ – 50 kΩ** (selectable) | **~50 kΩ** (software selectable on most pins) |
| Max sourcing current per pin | **40 mA** (absolute max) | **16 mA** (recommended ≤ 8 mA) |
| Typical digital input threshold | **> 3.0 V = HIGH**, **< 1.5 V = LOW** | **> 1.8 V = HIGH**, **< 0.8 V = LOW** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall for KTU Labs:** A 5 V signal from an Arduino-compatible sensor (e.g., HC-SR04 ultrasonic) wired directly into a Raspberry Pi GPIO will permanently destroy the Broadcom SoC. A **voltage level shifter** or a **resistor divider** must be used.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Engineering Problems of Digital Interfacing

When you connect a push button or a digital sensor to a microcontroller, three fundamental issues must be resolved *before* a single line of code is written:

#### Problem A — The Floating Input Problem

A digital input pin that is *not connected to anything* does **not** read 0 V. Instead, it picks up stray electromagnetic noise from the surrounding environment (mains 50 Hz hum, switching regulators, fluorescent lights) and oscillates randomly between HIGH and LOW. This is called a **floating input** or **high-impedance state**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Solution:** A *pull-up* or *pull-down* resistor forces the pin to a known default state when the switch is open. Arduino's internal pull-ups (20–50 kΩ) and Raspberry Pi's internal pull-ups (~50 kΩ) eliminate the need for an external resistor in most lab experiments.

#### Problem B — The Switch Bounce Problem

A mechanical push button is **not** a clean ideal switch. When the metal contacts close, they physically *bounce* against each other for **5 ms to 50 ms** before settling. During this bounce interval, the input pin sees a rapid stream of HIGH-LOW-HIGH-LOW transitions. A naive `if(digitalRead(pin)==HIGH) count++;` would register multiple "presses" for a single physical press.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Solutions:**
> - **Hardware debounce:** RC low-pass filter (τ = RC) + Schmitt trigger.
> - **Software debounce:** Time-stamp comparison — accept a transition only if the previous stable state was held for at least **t_debounce** (typically 20 ms).

#### Problem C — The Voltage-Level Mismatch Problem

A 5 V Arduino and a 3.3 V Raspberry Pi cannot exchange digital signals bidirectionally without level shifting. A *bidirectional* level shifter (e.g., BSS138 MOSFET-based module) is the standard solution.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ | Voltage divider (used as level shifter) | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $$\tau = R \cdot C$$ | RC time constant of debounce filter | seconds (s) |
| 3 | $$f_{sample} = \frac{1}{T_{sample}}$$ | Minimum polling frequency to avoid missing events | Hertz (Hz) |
| 4 | $$t_{debounce} \geq 5 \cdot \tau$$ | Time for RC signal to settle within ~1 % | seconds (s) |
| 5 | $$I_{pin} = \frac{V_{cc} - V_{OL}}{R_{pullup}}$$ | Current sunk by input pin in LOW state | Amperes (A) |
| 6 | $$P_{R} = I^2 \cdot R$$ | Power dissipated in pull-up resistor | Watts (W) |
| 7 | $$V_{IH} \geq 0.6 \cdot V_{cc}$$ | Guaranteed logic-HIGH input voltage (CMOS) | Volts (V) |
| 8 | $$V_{IL} \leq 0.3 \cdot V_{cc}$$ | Guaranteed logic-LOW input voltage (CMOS) | Volts (V) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition Reminder:** For an Arduino at $V_{cc} = 5\text{ V}$, the input thresholds are $V_{IH} \geq 3.0\text{ V}$ and $V_{IL} \leq 1.5\text{ V}$. For a Raspberry Pi at $V_{cc} = 3.3\text{ V}$, the thresholds are $V_{IH} \geq 2.0\text{ V}$ and $V_{IL} \leq 0.8\text{ V}$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In mechanical engineering practice, digital interfacing forms the backbone of:

- **CNC machine tool safety interlocks** (push-button ESTOP chains).
- **Automotive sensor networks** (Hall-effect wheel-speed sensors → ECU).
- **Robotics** (limit switches on robotic arms, IR line-follower sensors on AGVs).
- **Industrial SCADA** (PIR occupancy sensors for HVAC control).
- **Lab automation** (DS18B20 temperature logging in thermal test rigs).

A mechanical engineer who masters digital interfacing can rapidly prototype **mechatronic systems**, **data acquisition rigs**, and **closed-loop controllers** without depending on a dedicated electronics team.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Choosing a Pull-Up Resistor Value

**Problem Statement:** A push button is connected between a Raspberry Pi GPIO pin (3.3 V tolerant) and GND. An external pull-up resistor $R_{PU}$ holds the pin HIGH when the button is open. Determine the correct range of $R_{PU}$.

**Given Data:**
- $V_{cc} = 3.3\text{ V}$
- $V_{IL,max} = 0.8\text{ V}$ (maximum voltage still guaranteed as logic LOW)
- $I_{IL,max} = 1\text{ μA}$ (max leakage current the pin sources when LOW)
- Arduino-style worst-case sink current limit $I_{sink} = 40\text{ mA}$ (Raspberry Pi: only 16 mA; we use 8 mA design margin).

**Step 1 — Find the maximum allowable $R_{PU}$ (pull-up must not float).**

The worst case occurs when the button is *closed* and the pin pulls the line to LOW. The voltage at the pin must satisfy $V_{pin} \leq V_{IL,max}$.

Applying KVL in the closed state:

$$V_{cc} = I_{sink} \cdot R_{PU} + V_{pin}$$

Rearranging for $R_{PU,max}$:

$$R_{PU,max} = \frac{V_{cc} - V_{IL,max}}{I_{sink,min}} = \frac{3.3 - 0.8}{1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_{PU,max} = 2.5 \times 10^{6}\text{ Ω} = 2.5\text{ MΩ}$$

**Step 2 — Find the minimum allowable $R_{PU}$ (current-limit constraint).**

The smallest resistor that still keeps the sink current within the GPIO rating of 8 mA:

$$R_{PU,min} = \frac{V_{cc}}{I_{sink,max}} = \frac{3.3}{8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$R_{PU,min} = 412.5\text{ Ω}$$

**Step 3 — Choose the design value (KTU-recommended standard).**

A safe design midpoint on a log scale is the **10 kΩ** resistor — a universal industry default for hobby and lab interfacing.

$$R_{PU} = 10\text{ kΩ} \quad \text{(within } 412.5\text{ Ω} < R < 2.5\text{ MΩ)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Standard:** Use $R_{PU} = 10\text{ kΩ}$ (1 % tolerance, 0.25 W) for all digital input interfacing. This guarantees $V_{pin,LOW} \leq 0.33\text{ V} \ll 0.8\text{ V}$ and $I_{sink} = 0.33\text{ mA} \ll 8\text{ mA}$.

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Designing a Hardware Debounce Filter

**Problem Statement:** Design an RC low-pass filter to debounce a push button so that the output ripple after contact closure settles to within 5 % of $V_{cc}$ in 20 ms.

**Given:**
- $R = 10\text{ kΩ}$ (chosen to limit current)
- Settling time requirement: $t_{settle} \leq 20\text{ ms}$
- For a first-order RC step response, the output is:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{cc}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Relate the 5 % settling criterion to the time constant.**

Setting $V_{out}/V_{cc} = 0.95$:

$$0.95 = 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad e^{-t/\tau} = 0.05$$

Taking the natural log of both sides:

$$-\frac{t}{\tau} = \ln(0.05) = -2.996$$

$$\frac{t}{\tau} \approx 3 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t_{settle} \approx 3\tau$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\tau$ and then $C$.**

$$\tau = \frac{t_{settle}}{3} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 6.67 \times 10^{-3}\text{ s}$$

$$C = \frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{6.67 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{3}} = 6.67 \times 10^{-7}\text{ F}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 680\text{ nF} \;\; (\text{nearest E12 standard value})}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the design.**

- $\tau = 10\text{ kΩ} \cdot 680\text{ nF} = 6.8\text{ ms}$
- Settling time to 5 %: $3\tau = 20.4\text{ ms}$ ✓
- Bounce interval of a typical button (5–10 ms) is comfortably masked. ✓

### 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Voltage Divider for Level Shifting 5 V → 3.3 V

**Problem Statement:** A 5 V Arduino-compatible digital sensor must be safely read by a Raspberry Pi GPIO. Design a resistive voltage divider.

**Design Equations:**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

We need $V_{out} = 3.3\text{ V}$ when $V_{in} = 5.0\text{ V}$.

**Step 1 — Set the ratio.**

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{3.3}{5.0} = 0.66$$

**Step 2 — Choose $R_1$ and $R_2$ keeping total impedance between 1 kΩ and 10 kΩ** (high enough to avoid loading the sensor, low enough to avoid noise pickup).

Let $R_1 + R_2 = 5\text{ kΩ}$ → $R_2 = 3.3\text{ kΩ}$ and $R_1 = 1.7\text{ kΩ}$.

Using E12 standard values: $R_1 = 1.8\text{ kΩ}$, $R_2 = 3.3\text{ kΩ}$.

**Step 3 — Verify the output voltage.**

$$V_{out} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{3.3}{1.8 + 3.3} = 5.0 \cdot 0.647 = 3.24\text{ V} \;\;\checkmark$$

This sits comfortably in the $V_{IH} \geq 2.0\text{ V}$ window for the Raspberry Pi.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Polling Mode | Interrupt Mode |
|---|---|---|
| CPU wake-up frequency | Continuous loop | Only on event |
| Power consumption | High | Very low (sleep-friendly) |
| Latency | One loop-cycle delay | Microsecond ISR latency |
| Code complexity | Trivial | Requires `attachInterrupt()` / ISR |
| Risk of missed event | None (if loop fast) | High if interrupt not configured |
| KTU lab recommendation | Beginner experiments | Advanced labs with sleep modes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hardware Connection Block Diagram (Arduino UNO + Push Button + PIR Sensor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pwrUSB[5V USB Supply] --> vccRail[5V Rail]
    gndUSB[GND] --> gndRail[GND Rail]
    vccRail --> arduinoV[Arduino UNO 5V Pin]
    gndRail --> arduinoG[Arduino GND Pin]
    arduinoV --> arduinoCore[ATmega328P MCU Core]
    arduinoG --> arduinoCore
    arduinoCore --> d2[Digital Pin D2 INT0]
    arduinoCore --> d8[Digital Pin D8]
    arduinoCore --> d13[Digital Pin D13 LED Pin]
    d2 --> btnIn[Push Button NO Contact]
    btnIn --> btnR[10k Pull-down Resistor]
    btnR --> gndRail
    d8 --> pirV[PIR Sensor VCC]
    pirV --> pirCore[BISS0001 ASIC + Fresnel Lens]
    pirCore --> pirOut[PIR OUT Pin 3]
    pirOut --> d8
    pirOut --> gndRail
    d13 --> led[On-board LED]
    led --> gndRail
```

### 4.2 Firmware Execution Flow (Software Debounce Algorithm)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Power-on / Reset]) --> init[setup: pinMode INPUT_PULLUP, Serial.begin 9600]
    init --> loopStart([loop begins])
    loopStart --> readState[Read digitalRead pin2]
    readState --> compare{State changed since last read?}
    compare -- No --> loopStart
    compare -- Yes --> stamp[Save millis to debounceTimer]
    stamp --> wait[delay 20 ms blocking wait]
    wait --> reRead[Read digitalRead pin2 again]
    reRead --> confirm{Stable after 20 ms?}
    confirm -- No --> loopStart
    confirm -- Yes --> toggle[Execute action: count++, LED toggle, Serial.print]
    toggle --> loopStart
```

### 4.3 Raspberry Pi Voltage-Level Translation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    arduinoSide[Arduino 5V Domain] -->|5V HIGH/LOW| dividerR1[R1 = 1.8kΩ]
    dividerR1 --> dividerNode[Junction Node]
    dividerNode --> dividerR2[R2 = 3.3kΩ to GND]
    dividerNode -->|3.24V safe level| piGpio[Raspberry Pi GPIO Pin 17]
    arduinoSide -.->|GND reference| piGnd[Raspberry Pi GND]
    piGpio --> piCore[BCM2711 SoC Input Buffer]
    piCore --> piApp[Python RPi.GPIO event handler]
```

### 4.4 Signal Waveform Diagram (Bounce vs. Debounced)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ideal[Ideal Switch]
        i1[LOW] -->|press t0| i2[HIGH clean]
    end
    subgraph real[Real Switch with Bounce]
        r1[LOW] -->|bounce 0-10ms| r2[chattering HIGH-LOW-HIGH-LOW]
        r2 -->|settles 10ms| r3[stable HIGH]
    end
    subgraph debounced[After Software Debounce 20ms]
        d1[LOW] -->|wait 20ms| d2[single clean HIGH transition]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
*Explain the concept of "floating input" in a digital microcontroller pin. How is it resolved using a pull-up resistor?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A digital input pin that is not actively driven HIGH or LOW by any circuit element is said to be in a *floating* or *high-impedance* state. In this state, the pin picks up stray electromagnetic noise (50 Hz mains hum, switching transients) and the input logic level becomes undefined — the firmware sees random HIGH/LOW transitions.
The problem is resolved by connecting a *pull-up resistor* (typically **10 kΩ**) between the input pin and $V_{cc}$. When the push button is open, the resistor weakly holds the pin to a defined HIGH level. When the button is closed, it short-circuits the pin to GND, overriding the pull-up and producing a clean LOW. Most modern microcontrollers including the **ATmega328P** and **BCM2711** integrate this resistor internally, selectable via firmware (`pinMode(pin, INPUT_PULLUP)`).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
*What is switch bounce? List two methods to eliminate it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Switch bounce is the mechanical rebounding of metal contacts inside a push button or relay immediately after closure, producing a rapid stream of HIGH-LOW transitions lasting **5–50 ms** instead of a single clean edge.
Two methods to eliminate bounce:
1. **Hardware debounce:** An RC low-pass filter (e.g., $R = 10\text{ kΩ}$, $C = 100\text{ nF}$ giving $\tau = 1\text{ ms}$) followed by a Schmitt trigger (e.g., 74HC14) squares up the signal.
2. **Software debounce:** The firmware records the time of the last accepted state change using `millis()` and ignores any further transitions for a debounce interval of typically **20 ms** before validating the new state.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Push Button Interfacing with Arduino**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern, Module 2]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a push button connected to an Arduino UNO digital pin D2 using the internal pull-up resistor. Explain the active logic level when the button is pressed and released. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Circuit description:** Push button's one terminal is connected to **Arduino D2**; the other terminal is connected to **GND**. No external resistor is needed because the ATmega328P's internal pull-up (20–50 kΩ) is enabled in `pinMode(2, INPUT_PULLUP)`.
- **Active logic level:** The circuit is *active-LOW*. This means:
  - Button **open (released):** D2 reads **HIGH** (pulled to 5 V by internal resistor).
  - Button **closed (pressed):** D2 reads **LOW** (shorted to GND).
- **Pseudo-code for the test:**

```cpp
const int BTN = 2;
const int LED = 13;
void setup() {
    pinMode(BTN, INPUT_PULLUP);
    pinMode(LED, OUTPUT);
    Serial.begin(9600);
}
void loop() {
    int state = digitalRead(BTN);
    if (state == LOW) {
        digitalWrite(LED, HIGH);
        Serial.println("PRESSED");
    } else {
        digitalWrite(LED, LOW);
    }
}
```

- **Advantages of active-LOW:** Avoids floating input without external components; compatible with open-collector sensor outputs.

**(b)** Implement a counter that increments by 1 for every valid press of the push button, using a software debounce interval of 250 ms. Provide the complete Arduino sketch and explain the debounce logic. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```cpp
const int BTN = 2;
const int LED = 13;
int count = 0;
unsigned long lastDebounce = 0;
const unsigned long DEBOUNCE_MS = 250;
int lastStableState = HIGH;

void setup() {
    pinMode(BTN, INPUT_PULLUP);
    pinMode(LED, OUTPUT);
    Serial.begin(9600);
}

void loop() {
    int reading = digitalRead(BTN);
    if (reading != lastStableState) {
        lastDebounce = millis();
    }
    if ((millis() - lastDebounce) > DEBOUNCE_MS) {
        if (reading != lastStableState) {
            lastStableState = reading;
            if (lastStableState == LOW) {
                count++;
                digitalWrite(LED, !digitalRead(LED));
                Serial.print("Count = ");
                Serial.println(count);
            }
        }
    }
}
```

**Debounce logic explanation (4 sub-points for 7 marks):**
1. The loop continuously samples `digitalRead(BTN)` into the variable `reading`.
2. Whenever `reading` differs from `lastStableState`, the timer `lastDebounce` is reset to the current `millis()` value — this means the bounce window has restarted.
3. The condition `(millis() - lastDebounce) > DEBOUNCE_MS` is true only after 250 ms of stable reading.
4. After 250 ms of stable reading, the firmware commits the new state to `lastStableState` and, if it is LOW (press detected), increments `count` and toggles the LED.
5. A 250 ms window also doubles as a *minimum inter-press interval*, preventing contact-closure chattering from being interpreted as multiple presses.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Digital Sensor Interfacing with Raspberry Pi**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain how an **HC-SR04 ultrasonic distance sensor** (a 5 V device) can be interfaced safely to a **Raspberry Pi** (a 3.3 V device). Identify the specific GPIO pin risks and the level-shifting solution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Pin mapping of HC-SR04:** VCC (5 V), TRIG (input, 5 V TTL), ECHO (output, 5 V TTL), GND.
- **Risk identification:** The Raspberry Pi BCM2711 GPIO is **NOT 5 V tolerant**. Applying 5 V to any GPIO pin will forward-bias the on-chip ESD diode and permanently destroy the SoC. Therefore, the **ECHO line** must be level-shifted from 5 V down to 3.3 V.
- **TRIG line:** The Pi GPIO outputs 3.3 V. The HC-SR04 TRIG pin typically recognises any voltage > 1.5 V as HIGH, so the 3.3 V output is accepted directly — no level shift needed on TRIG.
- **ECHO level shift using a voltage divider:**

$$V_{echo,Pi} = 5\text{ V} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Choose $R_1 = 1\text{ kΩ}$ and $R_2 = 2\text{ kΩ}$:

$$V_{echo,Pi} = 5 \cdot \frac{2}{1 + 2} = 3.33\text{ V} \;\;\checkmark$$

This 3.33 V is safely read as HIGH by the Pi. Place $R_1$ on the HC-SR04 ECHO pin side, $R_2$ from junction node to GND, and connect junction node to Pi GPIO (e.g., GPIO 24).

**(b)** Write a Raspberry Pi Python script using the `RPi.GPIO` library that triggers the HC-SR04 every 500 ms, measures the echo pulse width, and prints the distance in centimetres. State the speed-of-sound formula used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Physics of distance measurement:**
The HC-SR04 emits a 40 kHz ultrasonic burst when TRIG is held HIGH for ≥ 10 µs. It then sets ECHO HIGH for a duration $t_{echo}$ proportional to the round-trip time of the sound wave.

$$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$$

where $v_{sound} \approx 343\text{ m/s}$ at 20 °C, giving:

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{t_{\text{echo,μs}}}{58.2}$$

(derivation: $343\text{ m/s} = 0.0343\text{ cm/μs}$; round-trip factor 2 → $0.0343/2 = 0.01715\text{ cm/μs}$; reciprocal = $58.2\text{ μs/cm}$.)

**Python script:**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

TRIG = 23
ECHO = 24
GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setup(TRIG, GPIO.OUT)
GPIO.setup(ECHO, GPIO.IN)

def get_distance_cm():
    GPIO.output(TRIG, GPIO.LOW)
    time.sleep(0.0002)
    GPIO.output(TRIG, GPIO.HIGH)
    time.sleep(0.00001)
    GPIO.output(TRIG, GPIO.LOW)
    while GPIO.input(ECHO) == 0:
        pulse_start = time.time()
    while GPIO.input(ECHO) == 1:
        pulse_end = time.time()
    pulse_duration = pulse_end - pulse_start
    distance = pulse_duration * 17150  # cm
    return round(distance, 2)

try:
    while True:
        d = get_distance_cm()
        print(f"Distance: {d} cm")
        time.sleep(0.5)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    GPIO.cleanup()
```

**Code walk-through (4 sub-points):**
1. The TRIG pin is pulsed HIGH for exactly 10 µs to command a new measurement.
2. Two `while` loops measure the leading and trailing edges of the ECHO pulse, capturing `pulse_start` and `pulse_end` timestamps.
3. The duration is multiplied by 17 150 (the constant derived from $v_{sound}/2$ in cm/s) to yield distance in cm.
4. The 500 ms sleep prevents acoustic interference between consecutive pings (HC-SR04 requires ≥ 60 ms cycle time).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where students lose 2–4 marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Confusing INPUT_PULLUP with INPUT.** Forgetting to enable the internal pull-up leaves the pin floating — the examiner will deduct **1 mark** for this omission.
> 2. **Wiring ECHO directly to Raspberry Pi.** A 5 V ECHO signal will destroy the Pi in real life and lose **2 marks** in the viva for not identifying the level-shifting requirement.
> 3. **Using $v_{sound} = 340\text{ m/s}$ without stating temperature correction.** KTU expects a mention of the temperature-dependence $v \approx 331 + 0.6T$ °C. Lose **1 mark** if omitted.
> 4. **Forgetting the `/2` factor in the ultrasonic distance formula.** The HC-SR04 measures *round-trip* time. This is a classic 1-mark trap.
> 5. **Failing to call `GPIO.cleanup()` in Python.** Examiners treat this as a sign of unprofessional code; lose **0.5 mark** for sloppy exit handling.
> 6. **Writing a 14-mark answer without any circuit diagram.** A pure-textual answer to a "draw the circuit" question loses **at least 2 marks** even if the explanation is perfect.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Digital input** = a binary signal (HIGH/LOW), not a continuous voltage.
- **Floating pin** = no defined state → solved by a **pull-up** (10 kΩ) or **pull-down** (10 kΩ) resistor, or the internal MCU pull-up enabled in firmware.
- **Arduino** is 5 V logic; **Raspberry Pi** is 3.3 V logic and is **NOT 5 V tolerant**.
- **5 V → 3.3 V level shift** uses a resistive voltage divider: $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot R_2/(R_1 + R_2)$.
- **Switch bounce** lasts 5–50 ms; eliminate it with **hardware RC + Schmitt trigger** or **software 20 ms delay**.
- **Active-LOW** wiring (button to GND, internal pull-up enabled) is the standard Arduino convention.
- **Polling** = read pin continuously; **Interrupt** = MCU wakes only on event.
- **HC-SR04** distance formula: $d_{\text{cm}} = t_{\text{echo,μs}} / 58.2$, derived from $v_{sound} = 343\text{ m/s}$ with round-trip factor 2.
- **PIR sensor** output is HIGH for a fixed delay (typically 3 s) on motion detection; no ranging required.
- **Hall-effect sensor** (e.g., A3144) is a digital sensor that outputs LOW in presence of a magnetic field > threshold.
- **DS18B20** temperature sensor uses the 1-Wire protocol — only one data line plus GND needed for digital temperature read-out.
- **Debounce rule of thumb:** wait at least **3 × τ** for an RC filter to settle within 5 % of final value.
- **Standard KTU lab resistor:** **10 kΩ pull-up**, **1.8 kΩ + 3.3 kΩ divider** for 5 V → 3.3 V.
- **Always call `GPIO.cleanup()`** at the end of any Raspberry Pi Python script.
- **Always state the formula** before plugging in numbers — KTU valuators award method marks even if the final number is wrong.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing DHT11 Sensor with Arduino or Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPL407 - COMPUTER AIDED MODELING AND SIMULATION OF MECHANICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Interfacing DHT11 Sensor with Arduino or Raspberry Pi # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **DHT11** is a calibrated, digital-output, low-cost relative humidity and temperature sensor that combines a **polymer-based capacitive humidity sensing element** with a **Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) thermistor** and an embedded 8-bit microcontroller that performs ADC conversion and single-wire serial data transmission. The sensor delivers a digital signal directly readable by any microcontroller unit (MCU) such as an **Arduino UNO** (Atmel ATmega328P, 8-bit AVR RISC architecture) or a **Raspberry Pi** (Broadcom BCM2837/BCM2711, ARM Cortex-A architecture). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (PCCPL407 – Module Experiment 2):** > This experiment is classified under *Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) data acquisition for mechanical system simulation*. The DHT11 acts as the physical transducer whose output is logged, processed, and visualized in a virtual simulation environment (e.g., MATLAB/Simulink, Python-MATLAB bridge) for analyzing thermal/fluidic behavior of mechanical systems. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the DHT11 as a **"digital nurse"** that constantly takes two readings — body **temperature** (via a tiny thermometer) and surrounding **moisture content** (via a moisture-detecting membrane) — and then shouts the numbers down a single telephone wire to a "doctor" (the Arduino/Raspberry Pi). Only one wire is used, so the "nurse" and "doctor" must take turns speaking, governed by a strict timing protocol — like a polite two-person conversation with hand-raise rules. > [!NOTE] > **Why only one wire?** The sensor integrates an onboard ASIC that converts analog resistance variations from the sensing elements into a clean digital pulse train. This eliminates the need for an external ADC on the host MCU and reduces the I/O pin count to a single bidirectional data line. ## 1.3 Key Physical/Electrical Specifications (Bolded Constants) | Parameter | Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Operating Voltage (Vcc) | **3.0 – 5.5** | V DC | | Max Current Draw | **2.5** | mA | | Temperature Range | **0 – 50** | °C | | Humidity Range | **20 – 90** | % RH | | Temperature Accuracy | **± 2** | °C | | Humidity Accuracy | **± 5** | % RH | | Resolution (Temp) | **1** | °C | | Resolution (RH) | **1** | % | | Sampling Period | **≥ 1** | second | | Output Signal | **Single-wire digital (open-drain)** | — | ## 1.4 Mechanical Engineering Relevance In mechanical systems, the DHT11 is widely used for: - **HVAC system** performance monitoring (chillers, AHUs). - **Environmental chamber** testing of components. - **Cold-chain logistics** for temperature-sensitive parts. - **Predictive maintenance** in compressor and turbine enclosures where humidity affects insulation. - **Smart factory (Industry 4.0)** digital-twin data ingestion. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle – Layered Functional Breakdown The DHT11 sensor stack is composed of four functional blocks: 1. **Capacitive Polymer Humidity Sensor (HS)** - A hygroscopic dielectric polymer absorbs/desorbs water vapor from ambient air. - The relative permittivity ($\varepsilon_r$) of the polymer changes linearly with %RH within 20–90 %. - Capacitance variation: $C_{HS} = f(RH) = C_0 \cdot (1 + \alpha \cdot RH)$, where $\alpha$ is the polymer sensitivity coefficient (typically $1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ per %RH). 2. **NTC Thermistor Temperature Sensor (TS)** - Resistance decreases exponentially with temperature, following the Steinhart–Hart equation. - $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$, where $\beta$ is the material constant (~ 3435 K for the DHT11 NTC bead). 3. **Front-End ASIC (Analog Front-End + ADC)** - Converts $C_{HS}$ to a frequency, then to a digital RH code. - Converts $R_T$ via a voltage divider to a digital temperature code. 4. **Single-Wire Serial Interface Controller** - Packages the two 8-bit values + checksum into a 40-bit data frame. ## 2.2 The Single-Wire Communication Protocol (Bidirectional, Open-Drain) The bus is **idle HIGH** through an external pull-up resistor (typically **10 kΩ**). Communication proceeds in three phases: **Phase A — Host Start Signal (MCU → Sensor):** The MCU pulls the line LOW for $\geq$ **18 ms**, then releases it and switches to input mode. The line is pulled HIGH by the pull-up. **Phase B — Sensor Response (Sensor → MCU):** After detecting the start, the sensor pulls the line LOW for **80 µs**, then HIGH for **80 µs** (synchronization/handshake). **Phase C — Data Transmission (40 bits):** Each bit is encoded by the duration of the LOW pulse followed by a HIGH pulse: - **Bit '0'**: LOW 50 µs + HIGH 26–28 µs - **Bit '1'**: LOW 50 µs + HIGH 70 µs ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` instead of `|` to keep markdown tables intact. | # | Equation / Quantity | Symbolic Form | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Capacitive RH model | $C_{HS} = C_0 \bigl(1 + \alpha \cdot RH\bigr)$ | Sensor calibration curve | | 2 | NTC Steinhart–Hart (simplified) | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \bigl(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\bigr)}$ | Temperature-to-resistance | | 3 | Voltage divider output | $V_{out} = V_{cc}\,\frac{R_T}{R_T + R_{ref}}$ | Analog read of thermistor | | 4 | Pull-up resistor sizing | $R_{pull} = \frac{V_{cc} - V_{OL}}{I_{OL}} \geq 4.7\,k\Omega$ | Bus rise-time constraint | | 5 | Data frame checksum | $CHK = (RHi + RHi\_dec + Ti + Ti\_dec) \bmod 256$ | Frame integrity check | | 6 | Sampling period constraint | $T_{sample} \geq 1\,s$ | Sensor self-heating limit | | 7 | RC bus rise-time | $\tau = R_{pull} \cdot C_{line}$ | Signal integrity design | | 8 | Heat dissipation (sensor body) | $P_{diss} = V_{cc} \cdot I_{cc} \approx 12.5\,mW$ | Self-heating error bound | ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Mechanical Engineering > [!NOTE] > In **digital-twin modeling of a refrigeration cycle**, the DHT11 provides real-time boundary-condition inputs (ambient T and RH) that are fed to a Simulink model of an evaporator. This makes the static model *dynamic* and validates predicted COP values against measured coefficients of performance. It is also used in: - **Internal combustion engine** intake air density compensation. - **Boiler/combustion chamber** psychrometric analysis. - **Cleanroom** particle-humidity correlation studies. - **Wind-tunnel** ambient condition logging. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: 40-Bit Data Frame Decoding The DHT11 transmits 40 bits in a single transaction. The frame structure (MSB first) is: $$ \text{Frame} = \underbrace{b_0 \cdots b_7}_{RHi\,\text{(Humidity Integer)}} \; \underbrace{b_8 \cdots b_{15}}_{RHd\,\text{(Humidity Decimal)}} \; \underbrace{b_{16} \cdots b_{23}}_{Ti\,\text{(Temp Integer)}} \; \underbrace{b_{24} \cdots b_{31}}_{Td\,\text{(Temp Decimal)}} \; \underbrace{b_{32} \cdots b_{39}}_{CHK} $$ **Step-by-step decoding logic:** 1. MCU initiates Start Signal: drives the data line LOW for $t_s = 18$ ms, then HIGH for $t_r = 30$ µs. 2. Sensor responds with handshake: $t_{ackL} = 80$ µs LOW, $t_{ackH} = 80$ µs HIGH. 3. Sensor transmits 40 data bits sequentially. For each bit, the MCU measures the duration of the HIGH portion, $t_{high}$. 4. Bit decision rule: $$ \text{bit} = \begin{cases} 0, & 26\,\mu s \leq t_{high} \leq 28\,\mu s \\ 1, & t_{high} \approx 70\,\mu s \end{cases} $$ 5. Reconstruct the 5 bytes using bit-shift accumulation: $$ \text{Byte}_k = \sum_{j=0}^{7} b_{8k+j} \cdot 2^{7-j} $$ 6. **Checksum verification** (mandatory — failing this means corrupted data): $$ CHK = (RHi + RHd + Ti + Td) \bmod 256 $$ 7. The accepted reading is valid only if $CHK_{received} = CHK_{calculated}$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: NTC Thermistor Linearization for Host MCU The raw resistance of the NTC bead is non-linear. The DHT11 ASIC internally linearizes it, but for analytical work we often need the relationship between the measured temperature code and the actual resistance. Starting from the Steinhart–Hart equation: $$ \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{\beta} \ln\!\left(\frac{R_T}{R_0}\right) $$ Convert to °C using $T\,[K] = T\,[°C] + 273.15$: $$ T\,[°C] = \left[\, \frac{1}{T_0\,[K]} + \frac{1}{\beta} \ln\!\left(\frac{R_T}{R_0}\right) \,\right]^{-1} - 273.15 $$ For a DHT11-class NTC: $R_0 = 10\,k\Omega$ at $T_0 = 25\,°C = 298.15\,K$, and $\beta = 3435\,K$. **Numerical example:** Suppose the voltage divider yields $V_{out} = 1.78\,V$ with $V_{cc} = 5\,V$ and $R_{ref} = 10\,k\Omega$. $$ R_T = R_{ref} \cdot \frac{V_{out}}{V_{cc} - V_{out}} = 10\,k\Omega \cdot \frac{1.78}{5 - 1.78} = 10\,k\Omega \cdot 0.5528 $$ $$ R_T \approx 5.528\,k\Omega $$ Plug into Steinhart–Hart: $$ \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{298.15} + \frac{1}{3435} \ln\!\left(\frac{5528}{10000}\right) $$ $$ \frac{1}{T} = 0.0033540 + 0.0002911 \cdot (-0.5922) $$ $$ \frac{1}{T} = 0.0033540 - 0.0001724 = 0.0031816\,K^{-1} $$ $$ T = 314.32\,K = 41.17\,°C $$ This 41.17 °C is the temperature of the sensor's NTC bead — the DHT11's internal ASIC subtracts a small self-heating correction (~ +0.5 °C) to report the ambient temperature. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pull-up Resistor Sizing The single-wire bus must transition from LOW to HIGH within a maximum time of ~ 30 µs (to satisfy the start of the sensor's response window). The RC time constant is governed by: $$ \tau = R_{pull} \cdot C_{line} $$ where $C_{line}$ includes the cable capacitance (~ 50 pF/m) plus the sensor's input capacitance (~ 30 pF). For a **20 cm jumper wire**: $C_{line} \approx 30\,pF + 10\,pF = 40\,pF$. To reach 90 % of $V_{cc}$ within $t = 15\,\mu s$ (half the budget): $$ 3\tau \leq 15\,\mu s \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; R_{pull} \leq \frac{15\,\mu s}{3 \cdot 40\,pF} = 125\,k\Omega $$ The standard practice is **$R_{pull} = 10\,k\Omega$**, giving $\tau = 0.4\,\mu s$ and $3\tau = 1.2\,\mu s$ — well within budget. This is the canonical KTU-recommended value. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Arduino vs Raspberry Pi Interfacing | Parameter | Arduino UNO (ATmega328P) | Raspberry Pi 4 (BCM2711) | |---|---|---| | Logic Level | **5 V** (tolerates 3.3 V) | **3.3 V** (NOT 5 V tolerant) | | Vcc to DHT11 | 5 V (with level-check) | 3.3 V (safer) | | Pull-up Voltage Reference | 5 V (use 4.7 kΩ if Vcc = 5 V) | 3.3 V (use 10 kΩ) | | Timing Source | Hardware timer / millis() | Software (less deterministic) | | OS Layer | Bare-metal / loop() | Linux with preemption | | Real-time Guarantees | Strong (no OS) | Weak (use libraries w/root) | | Best Library | Adafruit_DHT / DHT.h | Adafruit_CircuitPython_DHT | | Sampling Latency Jitter | **< 1 ms** | **5 – 20 ms** | | Recommended Use | Hard real-time logging | Data aggregation, cloud upload | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hardware Interfacing Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Host MCU: Arduino or Raspberry Pi] -->|GPIO pin
Bidirectional Data| B[DHT11 Sensor Module] B -->|Vcc 3.3 to 5 V| C[DC Power Rail] B -->|GND| D[Common Ground] E[Pull-up Resistor
10 kilo Ohm] -->|Connects Data line to Vcc| B A -->|Serial UART or USB| F[PC Visualization
Serial Monitor or Python] F -->|CSV or Stream| G[MATLAB or Simulink
Data Logger] G -->|Time-series Plot| H[Mechanical System
Performance Analysis] ``` ## 4.2 Signal Timing Flow on the Single-Wire Bus ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA[Idle: Line HIGH via pull-up] --> stepB[Host pulls line LOW for 18 ms] stepB --> stepC[Host releases line, waits 30 micro sec] stepC --> stepD[Sensor responds LOW 80 micro sec] stepD --> stepE[Sensor responds HIGH 80 micro sec] stepE --> stepF[Transmit Bit 0: LOW 50 micro sec, HIGH 26 to 28 micro sec] stepF --> stepG[Transmit Bit 1: LOW 50 micro sec, HIGH 70 micro sec] stepG --> stepH{All 40 bits sent?} stepH -->|No| stepF stepH -->|Yes| stepI[Line returns to IDLE HIGH] stepI --> stepJ[Host validates Checksum byte] stepJ -->|Match| stepK[Accept T and RH values] stepJ -->|Mismatch| stepL[Discard and retry after 1 second] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture for a Mechanical-System Use Case ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ENVIRONMENT_BLOCK [Mechanical System Environment] envA[Ambient Air at Inlet of AHU] envB[Compressor Cylinder Surface] end subgraph SENSING_BLOCK [DHT11 Sensing Module] s1[Capacitive Humidity Element] s2[NTC Thermistor Bead] s3[ASIC and ADC 8 bit] s4[Single-Wire TX Buffer] s1 --> s3 s2 --> s3 s3 --> s4 end subgraph HOST_BLOCK [Host Processor Block] h1[GPIO with Interrupt on Edge] h2[Timing Engine micro sec Resolution] h3[Frame Decoder and Checksum] h4[Numerical Filter Moving Avg] h1 --> h2 --> h3 --> h4 end subgraph VIRTUAL_BLOCK [Simulation and Visualization] v1[MATLAB Live Script or Python] v2[Simulink Real-time Model of HVAC] v3[Dashboard Gauge for T and RH] v4[Data Logger CSV to SD Card] v1 --> v2 v2 --> v3 v1 --> v4 end envA --> s1 envB --> s2 s4 -->|Single-wire digital| h1 h4 -->|Serial USB or MQTT| v1 ``` > [!NOTE] > The above Mermaid block has been drafted with purely alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters (e.g., `stepA`, `envA`, `s1`). No reserved keywords are used as standalone IDs, and no markdown bold/italic tags appear inside the node labels. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` **Define the DHT11 sensor and state its three primary functional blocks.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The DHT11 is a **digital-output, single-wire relative humidity and temperature sensor** that combines (i) a **resistive-type NTC thermistor** for temperature measurement, (ii) a **capacitive polymer humidity sensor** for relative humidity measurement, and (iii) an **8-bit microcontroller ASIC** that digitizes the analog signals, performs checksum calculation, and transmits the 40-bit data frame over a single bidirectional data line. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **What is the function of the pull-up resistor in a DHT11 interfacing circuit, and what is its standard value?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **pull-up resistor (typically 10 kΩ)** keeps the single-wire data line at a defined HIGH logic level (Vcc) when no device is actively driving the bus. The line uses an **open-drain** configuration, meaning the DHT11 and the host MCU can only pull the line LOW — they cannot drive it HIGH. Without the pull-up, the bus would float, causing undefined logic levels. The 10 kΩ value balances a fast RC rise time with low current consumption. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Module Question (Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model]` **(a)** Draw and explain the **single-wire communication protocol** of the DHT11 sensor, including the host start signal, sensor response, and the timing diagram for data bit '0' and '1'. State the duration of each timing interval. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Interface the DHT11 with an **Arduino UNO**. Draw the **circuit diagram**, list the **pin connections**, and describe (in words/flowchart form) the **algorithm** to read temperature and humidity and display them on the Serial Monitor. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks **Step 1 (2 marks):** The protocol is a **single-wire bidirectional serial format** with three phases: - **Phase 1 — Host Start:** MCU drives line LOW for **$t_s \geq 18$ ms**, then releases and switches to input mode. (½ mark) - **Phase 2 — Sensor Response:** After detecting the start (rising edge), the DHT11 first pulls the line LOW for **80 µs**, then HIGH for **80 µs**. This is the "acknowledge" pattern. (1 mark) - **Phase 3 — Data Transmission:** Sensor transmits **40 bits** MSB-first. Each bit begins with a fixed **LOW of 50 µs**, followed by a variable HIGH whose duration encodes the bit. (½ mark) **Step 2 (2 marks):** Bit encoding rules: $$ \text{Bit '0'} \rightarrow \text{LOW 50}\mu s + \text{HIGH 26 to 28}\mu s $$ $$ \text{Bit '1'} \rightarrow \text{LOW 50}\mu s + \text{HIGH 70}\mu s $$ The MCU measures $t_{high}$ and applies the decision rule (threshold ~ 50 µs). (2 marks) **Step 3 (2 marks):** Frame structure (40 bits): $$ \underbrace{RHi}_{8} \;\underbrace{RHd}_{8} \;\underbrace{Ti}_{8} \;\underbrace{Td}_{8} \;\underbrace{CHK}_{8} $$ Checksum rule: $CHK = (RHi + RHd + Ti + Td) \bmod 256$. (2 marks) **Step 4 (1 mark):** Mention sampling constraint: a **minimum 1-second interval** between consecutive reads to avoid self-heating errors. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks **Step 1 (2 marks):** Circuit diagram (described in text since code/Mermaid is restricted): | DHT11 Pin | Arduino UNO Pin | |---|---| | Vcc | **5 V** (or 3.3 V for safety) | | GND | **GND** | | DATA | **Digital Pin D2** | | DATA (pull-up) | **10 kΩ** resistor between DATA and Vcc | (2 marks) **Step 2 (2 marks):** Algorithm in pseudocode-form (NO actual code, per KTU rules): > **Begin** *every 2 seconds*: > 1. **Set** digital pin D2 as OUTPUT, drive LOW, wait 18 ms. > 2. **Set** D2 as INPUT (release line). > 3. **Wait** for rising edge, then falling edge (sensor response). > 4. **Measure** time of subsequent HIGH pulses using a microsecond timer. > 5. **Decode** each bit: if $t_{high} > 50\,\mu s$ → bit = 1, else bit = 0. > 6. **Accumulate** 40 bits into 5 bytes. > 7. **Verify** $CHK = (RHi + RHd + Ti + Td) \bmod 256$. > 8. **If** checksum matches, **print** "Humidity = RHi.RHd % , Temperature = Ti.Td °C" to Serial Monitor; else **discard** and retry after 1 s. (2 marks) **Step 3 (2 marks):** Discussion points (must include for full marks): - Use of the **Adafruit DHT library** to abstract low-level timing (½ mark). - Configure Serial Monitor at **9600 baud** (½ mark). - Add a **2-second delay** between readings to respect sensor sampling rate (½ mark). - The whole system is a **hardware-in-the-loop data acquisition** front-end for a mechanical-system simulation. (½ mark) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Alternative]` **(a)** Explain the **internal sensing mechanism** of the DHT11 — covering the capacitive polymer humidity element and the NTC thermistor. Derive the Steinhart–Hart equation and compute the temperature if the NTC resistance is **5.528 kΩ**, given $R_0 = 10\,k\Omega$, $T_0 = 25\,°C$, and $\beta = 3435\,K$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Interface the DHT11 with a **Raspberry Pi 4** and explain the steps for installing the required **Python library**, reading the sensor, and logging the data to a CSV file. Draw the connection diagram. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks **Step 1 (2 marks):** Humidity mechanism: - Polymer layer absorbs water vapor → its relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ changes → capacitance changes linearly with RH: $C = C_0(1 + \alpha \cdot RH)$, $\alpha \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ per %RH. (1 mark) - This capacitance is converted to a frequency in the ASIC, then to a digital RH code. (1 mark) **Step 2 (1 mark):** Temperature mechanism: - NTC thermistor's resistance decreases as temperature increases. Resistance follows the **Steinhart–Hart equation**: $$ \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{\beta} \ln\!\left(\frac{R_T}{R_0}\right) $$ **Step 3 (3 marks):** Numerical computation with $R_T = 5.528\,k\Omega$, $R_0 = 10\,k\Omega$, $T_0 = 298.15\,K$, $\beta = 3435\,K$: $$ \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{298.15} + \frac{1}{3435} \ln\!\left(\frac{5.528}{10}\right) $$ $$ = 0.0033540 + 0.0002911 \times (-0.5922) $$ $$ = 0.0033540 - 0.0001724 = 0.0031816\,K^{-1} $$ $$ T = 314.32\,K = 41.17\,°C $$ (Show all arithmetic steps for full marks — see Section 3.2 for verification.) **Step 4 (1 mark):** Discussion: - The DHT11's internal ASIC applies a small self-heating correction (~ +0.5 °C offset), so the reported ambient temperature is approximately 40.67 °C. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks **Step 1 (2 marks):** Pin connection table: | DHT11 Pin | Raspberry Pi 4 Pin | |---|---| | Vcc | **Pin 1 (3.3 V)** | | GND | **Pin 6 (GND)** | | DATA | **GPIO4 (Pin 7)** | | Pull-up | **10 kΩ** between DATA and 3.3 V | (2 marks) **Step 2 (2 marks):** Library installation steps (described in plain text per KTU rules): 1. Update the package list: `sudo apt update`. 2. Install pip and the Adafruit DHT library. 3. Install build-essential and Python development headers. 4. Clone the Adafruit Python DHT Sensor Library repository. 5. Navigate to the library folder and build it. 6. Run a sample script that imports the DHT module and reads from GPIO4. (2 marks) **Step 3 (2 marks):** Logging workflow (described as a numbered list, NO executable code): 1. Open a CSV file in **append mode** with columns: `timestamp, temperature_C, humidity_RH`. 2. Read the sensor using the library's read function with a retry loop (up to 15 retries since the Pi's Linux scheduler is non-deterministic). 3. If reading is non-null, format the timestamp using `datetime.now()`. 4. Write a single row to the CSV. 5. Sleep for **2 seconds** before the next iteration to respect the 1 Hz sampling constraint of the DHT11. 6. Wrap the read operation in a `try/except` block to handle intermittent I/O errors. (2 marks) **Step 4 (1 mark):** Engineering note: - Raspberry Pi GPIO is **3.3 V only**; do **not** connect 5 V to its pins — this will damage the SoC. Hence Vcc of the DHT11 must be the 3.3 V rail. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make (and where marks are lost):** > 1. **Confusing the data format** — Writing the DHT11's frame as 5 bytes of 8 bits each is correct (40 bits), but some students mistakenly claim 32 bits. (-2 marks) > 2. **Ignoring the checksum** — Failing to mention the checksum verification step loses at least 1 mark in protocol questions. (-1 mark) > 3. **Wrong pull-up value** — Stating 1 kΩ (too small, wastes current) or 100 kΩ (too slow rise time). The correct answer is 4.7 kΩ – 10 kΩ; 10 kΩ is the standard. (-1 mark) > 4. **Wrong voltage for Raspberry Pi** — Applying 5 V to the Pi's GPIO is a destructive error. Always emphasize 3.3 V. (-2 marks) > 5. **Missing the 1-second sampling constraint** — The DHT11 cannot be polled faster than 1 Hz; mention this in any data-acquisition question. (-1 mark) > 6. **Confusing NTC with PTC** — A common slip; remember the "N" in NTC stands for "Negative" — resistance falls as temperature rises. (-1 mark) > 7. **Forgetting the 18 ms start signal** — A common oversight; the host must hold the line LOW for at least 18 ms (some sources say 20 ms). (-1 mark) --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **DHT11** = digital, single-wire, calibrated T + RH sensor. - Operating range: **0 – 50 °C, 20 – 90 % RH**, accuracy ± 2 °C / ± 5 % RH. - **Vcc**: 3 – 5.5 V; **DATA**: open-drain, requires a **10 kΩ pull-up**. - **Sampling rate**: at least **1 second** between reads. - **Protocol**: 3 phases — Start (≥ 18 ms LOW by MCU), Response (80 µs LOW + 80 µs HIGH by sensor), Data (40 bits). - **Bit '0'**: HIGH = 26–28 µs; **Bit '1'**: HIGH = 70 µs. - **Frame**: 40 bits = 8-bit RHi + 8-bit RHd + 8-bit Ti + 8-bit Td + 8-bit Checksum. - **Checksum**: $CHK = (RHi + RHd + Ti + Td) \bmod 256$. - **Humidity sensor**: capacitive polymer; capacitance $C = C_0(1 + \alpha RH)$. - **Temperature sensor**: NTC thermistor; Steinhart–Hart equation. - **Arduino UNO** = 5 V logic, real-time, ideal for hard real-time logging. - **Raspberry Pi** = 3.3 V logic, NOT 5 V tolerant, suitable for aggregation/cloud upload. - **Pull-up sizing**: $R_{pull} \leq \dfrac{15\,\mu s}{3 \cdot C_{line}}$; standard value is 10 kΩ. - **Mechanical-engineering uses**: HVAC, cold-chain, AHU, cleanroom, IC-engine intake compensation, digital-twin data ingestion. - **Library choice (Arduino)**: `DHT.h`; **(Raspberry Pi)**: `Adafruit_DHT` Python package. - **Engineering caution**: never exceed 1 read per second; otherwise sensor self-heating introduces a positive bias in temperature. - **Mechanical simulation link**: feed DHT11 readings into Simulink/MATLAB to provide real boundary conditions for HVAC and refrigeration digital twins.
